document.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Inside Eligibility and Entitlement Using Cúram Express Rules Guide

root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Inside Eligibility and Entitlement Using Cúram Express Rules Guide
root.summary: Front matter and reference content for the Cúram 8.1.2 “Inside Eligibility and Entitlement Using Cúram Express Rules” guide. It introduces the guide’s purpose and audience, then acts as a roadmap to the main chapter covering eligibility/entitlement processing, determination navigation, calculation and display of eligibility/decision details, rule object converters/propagators, storage, financial scheduling, reassessment, incremental design, compliancy, engine API/customizability, CER extensions, caching, and environment variables. It also includes the guide’s edition scope (Cúram 8.1–8.1.2) and legal notices, copyright, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful mainly as a navigation and legal reference node, not substantive implementation content.

children.title: Note
children.summary: This note directs readers to review the Notices section on page 243 before using the product information. It also shows the copyright notice for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Front matter and chapter opener for the Cúram 8.1–8.1.2 “Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by using Cúram Express Rules” guide. Includes edition/copyright pages, a detailed table of contents, related reading, and chapter list. Introduces the guide’s purpose, audience, and the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine’s role in assessing cases with CER. Also gives a high-level processing overview: configure product, gather input, calculate determination, store/retrieve results, schedule financials, and reassess when circumstances change.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: Introduces eligibility and entitlement processing in Cúram Express Rules: product configuration, evidence and input data recording, precedent change sets, dependency-driven reactive recalculation, and how determination results are built, stored, and used. Explains the “three Es” (eligibility, entitlement, explanation), fixed vs product-specific rules, active vs reactive determinations, and a Lone Parent Benefit example showing changing eligibility over time. Also covers determination storage tables and the financial scheduler’s role in turning assessment determinations into financial schedules.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-34
children.summary: Explains how Cúram Engine turns determination results into financial schedules and how case workers view determinations. Covers current, historical, snapshot, manual check, and reassessment determinations, including in-edit evidence handling. Describes coverage periods and “vertical slice” decision views. The main focus is eligibility and entitlement calculation: fixed Engine layers, product periods, custom rule classes, rate/custom entity/custom evidence data, and the immutable DeterminationResult that is spliced across product periods and displayed as coverage-period summaries.

children.title: Edition — pages 35-44
children.summary: Explains eligibility/entitlement concepts and how the Engine divides a case lifetime into immutable coverage periods based on changes in eligibility, entitlement, or decision details. Covers how to define and implement product structure rule classes: Product, Objective Type, and Objective Tag Type, including key inherited attributes and default wizard-generated classes. Also outlines analysis steps for mapping legislation/product periods, objectives, frequencies, and supported tags, plus how to relate product → objective types → tag types in the rule set.

children.title: Edition — pages 45-54
children.summary: This section explains how to implement and wire Cúram Express Rules for eligibility/entitlement: case rule classes, objective instance rule classes, and objective tag rule classes, including inherited attributes, naming, and timeline-based logic. It describes how to create objective lists with fixed/dynamic lists, pass context, and use timelines in calculations. It then covers setting up product periods in development or a running system, and finally how to configure or customize determination summarizer strategies for entitlement summaries.

children.title: Edition — pages 55-64
children.summary: Edition section (pp. 55–64) covers advanced CER product configuration and the deprecated key decision factors feature. It explains testing and troubleshooting for eligibility/entitlement, including missing product periods and runtime rule errors. It then introduces key decision factors: what they are, how they are displayed graphically/list-wise, how the Engine calculates and stores them across product periods, and how to analyze, design, and implement them. It details factor cardinality, descriptions, named key events, rule set layering, and the required case rule class structure.

children.title: Edition — pages 65-74
children.summary: Deprecated key decision factors setup for Cúram Express Rules: how to define key decision factor rule classes extending AbstractKeyDataTimeline, optional keyEvents, and when to implement timeline or leave defaults. Explains naming, context attributes, null-date event handling, and linking key decision factors to a case via keyDataTimelines using fixed/dynamic/joined lists. Also covers DMX updates to connect product periods to rule classes. The latter pages introduce decision details: display categories, multi-locale support, screen behavior, comparison/subscreens, and high-level calculation flow.

children.title: Edition — pages 75-84
children.summary: Covers Cúram decision details implementation for product periods and display categories. Explains custom rule classes for top-level case details and sub-screens, using Display/DisplaySubscreen annotations and optional calculator rules. Describes how the Engine walks rule objects, timelines, lists, and sub-screen businessObjectID data to build XML timelines in DeterminationResult, and how Dynamic UIM pages query that XML via supported server interfaces. Includes basic eligibility/entitlement XML/UIM examples and an overview of analysis steps, concepts, and how decision details differ from eligibility/entitlement.

children.title: Edition — pages 85-94
children.summary: Explains how to implement decision details display categories for Cúram eligibility/entitlement: identify categories and screen layouts, map displayed data to underlying calculation results, define keys for expandable sub-screens, and determine any comparison data from previous decisions. Covers implementation of case rule classes, display and DisplaySubscreen attributes, sub-screen wrapper classes, Dynamic UIM top-level and sub-screen pages, and DMX configuration for display categories and rule class links. Also notes best practices like separating decision-details rules from eligibility rules and the deprecated key decision factors feature.

children.title: Edition — pages 95-104
children.summary: Covers decision details setup/testing and starts the chapter on rule object converters and propagators. It explains how to configure display categories and product periods for a running system, publish changes, and test Dynamic UIM/decision details XML and CER errors. Then it introduces converters/propagators, showing how CER retrieves business data as rule objects and how the Dependency Manager tracks changes for reassessment. It begins the initial assessment example and details product delivery rule objects, conversion of case data, and reassessment strategy behavior for closed cases.

children.title: Edition — pages 105-114
children.summary: Covers CER rule object propagation for Rate Rule Objects and Entity Rule Objects. Rate data uses propagator type ROPT2006, is stored in CER tables, propagated only on full/initial or explicit apply changes, top-level cells only, and ignores range data. Entity data uses ROPT2003 to convert/propagate CRUD-style rows into rule objects, with configuration, filtering, logical deletion, precedent tracking, and restrictions on access. Also details supported entity operation stereotypes, non-propagatable operation handling, excluded tables, and reuse guidance for rule classes.

children.title: Edition — pages 115-124
children.summary: Introduces Active Succession Set Rule Objects for eligibility/entitlement. Explains how active evidence succession sets are converted into CER rule objects, how the propagator notifies the Dependency Manager on evidence changes, and how configuration works (ROPT2005, evidence types, target rule classes). Covers timeline vs non-timeline attribute population, lifetime start/end via annotations, parent/child succession relationships, inherited attributes like successionID/caseID/exists/evidenceDescriptorID, restrictions on <readall>/<match> access, and precedents/propagation behavior. Includes an Income evidence example with activation and resulting rule object values.

children.title: Edition — pages 125-134
children.summary: Explains Active Evidence Row Rule Objects in CER: how active evidence rows are converted to rule objects, configured via ROPT2004 mappings, and how parent/child evidence relationships are populated. Covers inherited attributes, handling of in-edit changes, and access restrictions on <readall>/<match> (caseID only). Also describes precedents and recalculation triggers for active evidence and data configurations, plus propagation behavior when evidence is created, corrected, changed, or canceled. Includes an Income/Responsibility example contrasting row-based evidence with succession-set timelines.

children.title: Edition — pages 135-144
children.summary: Explains CER rule object conversion and propagation for active evidence, using Income evidence examples to show activation, change, end, cancellation, and reassessment through Dependency Manager. Introduces child evidence with Responsibility linked to parent Income and navigation between parent/child rule objects. Covers configuration error handling (warn/ignore/error), validation-on-load, normal vs in-edit evidence access, incremental propagation limits, bulk rate rule object maintenance, tracing/logging controls, supported domain-to-CER type mappings, and propagator filter hooks.

children.title: Edition — pages 145-154
children.summary: Introduces a propagation filtering hook for eligibility/entitlement reassessment control, including the `PropagatorFilterHook` interface, how multiple implementations and propagator types are bound, and notes on database details available to the hook. The main topic is how the Engine stores determinations: `CREOLECaseDetermination`, XML data, optional rule-object snapshots, and linked `CaseDecision`/objective/tag tables. It explains decision periods, superseding behavior, and which eligibility/entitlement changes create stored records, plus comparison strategies for deciding whether a new determination is different enough to save.

children.title: Edition — pages 155-164
children.summary: This section covers how the Engine stores determination snapshots and how products choose or implement a determination comparison strategy, including built-in options and custom strategy wiring. It then introduces financial scheduling: how eligible case decisions and case deductions are converted into financial components and instruction line items. Key factors include case decision objectives, nominee component assignments, delivery patterns, cover periods, open-ended cases, and deduction types. Useful for understanding eligibility/entitlement persistence and the rules/configuration needed to generate payments and deductions.

children.title: Edition — pages 165-174
children.summary: Explains how Cúram schedules financials for case deduction items and payment corrections. Covers deduction cover periods, required case/nominee setup, overpayment/underpayment/net-zero correction types, correction products, and nominee handling. Also describes how reassessments are triggered and aggregated: case-level reassessment via Dependency Manager, Reassessment Aggregation workflow, queue/control tables, deferred processing, and diagnostics. Ends with bulk reassessment for system-wide changes like product configuration, CER rules, rule object data, and rate tables.

children.title: Edition — pages 175-184
children.summary: Covers how system-wide changes affecting CER-based eligibility/entitlement cases are published and then bulk reassessed. Describes affected change types: product configuration/product periods, CER rule sets, configurable rule object data, and rate tables, including sandbox/publish or Apply Changes behavior. Explains bulk reassessment goals (consistency, efficiency, concurrency, business-specific control), how reassessment works, and trade-offs between Dependency Manager bottom-up processing and custom/top-down algorithms. Includes benefits/limitations of each approach and introduces the sample CREOLEBulkCaseChunkReassessmentByProduct batch process.

children.title: Edition — pages 185-194
children.summary: Covers bulk reassessment for CER-based cases and operational guidance: implementing custom chunker/streamer batch processes, using DeterminationCalculator.hasDecisionChanged, handling multiple simultaneous system-wide changes, scheduling and parallel execution behavior, and diagnostics via DependencyTrace/system logs. Also introduces incremental design and evolution: how CER rule sets depend on each other, how to clone application rule sets and update DMX/XML resources, and practical shortcuts/defaults for starting a product with open-ended cases and total weekly monetary entitlement.

children.title: Edition — pages 195-204
children.summary: Guidance for implementing eligibility/entitlement products in CER: start with a single product period, hard-code core eligibility/entitlement rules first, and separate reusable data rule classes, calculators, and rate data into distinct rule sets. It recommends a top-down approach for eligibility logic, bottom-up for complex calculators, and initially hard-coding rates. The section also covers implementation order for decision details and explains two ways to handle legislation changes—branching logic vs multiple product periods—comparing their effects on testing, maintenance, complexity, display categories, and case timing.

children.title: Edition — pages 205-214
children.summary: Covers product-level eligibility/entitlement configuration changes and their impact: legislation timing, decision summary display, determination comparison, open-ended cases, and reassessment strategy (including batch job ApplyProductReassessmentStrategy). Defines compliancy rules: public API protection, restricted Java packages/code tables, and database table access limits. Describes the Engine API, customization via events and hooks, and the ProductDeliveryApprovalStrategyHook for custom effective dates. Introduces CER extensions, especially combineSuccessionSets, with an absence-history example.

children.title: Edition — pages 215-224
children.summary: This section covers two CER expressions: combineSuccessionSets and legislationChange. It explains their behavior, validation/runtime constraints, default values for missing succession-set gaps, and XML structure. It also gives worked examples: combining Absence evidence into a CombinedAbsence timeline and determining eligibility from changing income-based legislation eras. Includes rule-class setup, annotations, and sample XML for Person, Absence, and CombinedAbsence classes.

children.title: Edition — pages 225-234
children.summary: This section explains the CER “rate” expression and the Engine annotations Display, DisplaySubscreen, Legislation, and SuccessionSetPopulation. It shows how rate tables are referenced by table/row/column codes, how rate values vary over time, fallback behavior when propagated rule objects are missing, and the requirement for a database transaction during evaluation. It also includes XML examples for using rate in eligibility logic and for each annotation’s syntax and purpose.

children.title: Edition — pages 235-242
children.summary: This section documents rule object annotations for automatically populating related evidence and related succession set attributes, including parent/child relationship examples and restrictions on applicable rule classes and return types. It then explains EvidenceDescriptor transaction caching during reassessment, default behavior, how to disable it, and the three cache keys involved. Finally, it lists environment variables that control engine tracing, rule object propagation, batch reassessment behavior, workflow ticket generation, caching, and suppression of specific evidence methods.

Front matter and a full guide to Cúram 8.1–8.1.2 eligibility and entitlement with Cúram Express Rules. Covers the guide’s purpose, audience, processing flow, determination storage/retrieval, financial scheduling, reassessment (case-level and bulk), incremental design, compliancy/API/customizability, CER extensions (expressions and annotations), caching, and environment variables. Includes detailed examples and implementation guidance for product configuration, decision details, rule object converters/propagators, and deprecated key decision factors.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: Table of contents for the guide “Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by using Cúram Express Rules.” Lists Chapter 1 topics and page references, including eligibility/entitlement processing overview, navigating determinations, displaying eligibility and decision details, rule object converters/propagators, storage, financial scheduling, reassessment, incremental design, compliancy, engine API/customizability, CER extensions, caching, environment variables, and notices. Useful as a navigation index rather than substantive content.

children.title: 1 Developing with Eligibility and Entitlement by using Cúram Express Rules
1.1 Introduction
Purpose
children.summary: Explains the guide’s purpose: to show how case eligibility and entitlement are assessed using Cúram Express Rules and how to set up the application to return those results. It emphasizes example-driven, staged learning—from basic eligible periods and entitlement amounts to more complex results with detailed derivations. It also notes the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine is the processing component behind assessments, and that the content is aimed at technical readers, designers/developers, and operators handling case reassessment.

children.title: Audience
children.summary: Intended for a technical audience, this section says the guide helps designers, developers, and system operators understand how the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine fits into case processing. It is useful for building and customizing products, learning CER-based eligibility/entitlement assessment, and understanding operational needs such as bulk case reassessment.

children.title: Related Reading
children.summary: Lists related Cúram documents for background and configuration guidance, including Integrated Case Management, case configuration, product building, Dynamic Evidence, and Express Rules. Also gives an overview of the guide’s chapters: eligibility and entitlement processing, navigating determinations, calculating/displaying eligibility and entitlement, deprecated key decision factors, decision details, and rule object converters/propagators. Useful as a navigation hub to find supporting materials or jump to specific chapters.

children.title: Chapters in this Guide
children.summary: This section lists the guide’s chapters and what each covers. It points to an overview of eligibility and entitlement processing, navigation of determinations, calculation and display of eligibility/entitlement, deprecated key decision factors, decision details, and rule object converters/propagators. It helps readers quickly choose the chapter relevant to understanding Engine processing, case worker views, and CER rule integration.

Introduces the guide to using Cúram Express Rules for case eligibility and entitlement. Explains the two goals: understand how the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine assesses cases and learn how to configure the application to return results. Emphasizes staged, example-driven learning from basic eligibility/entitlement outputs to detailed derivations. Identifies the technical audience: designers, developers, and system operators, including bulk reassessment needs. Also lists related Cúram documents and previews the guide’s chapters.

children.title: 1.2 Eligibility and Entitlement Processing at a Glance
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine and its role with Cúram Express Rules in determining case eligibility/entitlement over a case’s lifetime. Explains the overall processing flow: configure product, gather input, calculate determinations, store results, schedule financials, retrieve history, assess initially, and reassess when circumstances or product rules change. Highlights the Dependency Manager’s role in tracking changes and triggering deferred reassessment. Also notes that later sections provide more detail and an example story.

children.title: Product Configuration
Example
children.summary: Introduces product configuration for eligibility and entitlement using Cúram Express Rules. Explains that agencies must define products, evidence types, input data, business rules, and product periods. Uses a “Lone Parent” Benefit example to show configuring evidence, eligibility/entitlement/explanation rules, and Rule Object data for rules processing. Notes that once configured, case workers can create product delivery cases. Begins a new topic on recording input data changes that can affect decisions, including new evidence, corrections, and product-wide data.

Explains how an agency configures a product for eligibility and entitlement processing in Cúram Express Rules. Key setup includes evidence types, input data, business rules for eligibility/entitlement/explanations, and product periods for legislation changes. Uses a “Lone Parent” Benefit example showing evidence, eligibility rules, and Rule Object data to expose personal details to CER. Notes that once configured, case workers can create product delivery cases. Also begins the topic of recording input data changes that affect decisions, including new evidence, corrections, and product-wide data.

children.title: Recording of Input Data
Example
children.summary: Explains how Cúram tracks data changes that may affect eligibility/entitlement decisions using precedent change sets and the Dependency Manager. It describes when precedent items are created for case creation, personal details, activated evidence, and product-wide configuration changes. Most changes trigger deferred recalculation processes; product configuration changes are processed in batch. The example shows a father’s personal details being recorded, creating a precedent change set item, but no recalculation occurs because no dependencies exist yet.

Explains how Cúram records input data changes that can affect eligibility/entitlement. It identifies when precedent change set items are created for product delivery cases, pertinent personal details, activated evidence, and product-wide configuration updates. Most such changes trigger deferred recalculation using Dependency Manager dependencies; product-wide configuration changes run in batch. Includes an example where recording a father’s personal details creates a precedent change set item, but no recalculation occurs because no dependents exist yet.

children.title: Rules Calculations and Determination Results
What a Determination Result Contains
The Three Es: Eligibility, Entitlement, and Explanation
children.summary: Explains the “three Es” in CER determination results: eligibility (yes/no for the claim), entitlement (what the claimant receives, only during eligible periods), and explanation (case-worker-facing reasons and decision details). Describes how a product delivery case accumulates registered members and evidence, triggering deferred processing and later active evidence. Notes that determination results are built from case data into rule objects, and that eligibility/entitlement are largely driven by legislation/policy, while explanation requirements are more flexible and presentation-focused.

children.title: The Determination Result Covers the Lifetime of a Case
children.summary: This section explains that eligibility, entitlement, and explanation are determined over the full lifetime of a case, from its start date to end date. It defines how actual and expected start/end dates are used, notes that product delivery cases may be open-ended, and describes implications for open-ended decisions and financial schedules. It also distinguishes past versus future periods, explains reassessment when dates change, and notes that eligibility may remain constant while explanation changes.

Defines what a CER determination result contains: the “three Es” of eligibility (overall yes/no), entitlement (what the claimant receives, only during eligible periods), and explanation (case-worker-facing reasons, decision factors, and display details). It notes that results are built from case input data into rule objects. The section also explains that determination applies across a case’s full lifetime, including open-ended cases, past vs future periods, and reassessment when start/end dates change; explanation may vary even when eligibility does not.

children.title: What Triggers the Calculation of a Determination Result
Active Determination Calculation Requests
children.summary: Explains when the Engine explicitly requests CER to calculate a determination result. Covers four active triggers: prior to evidence activation (interim “what if” requests, no dependency storage), approval (snapshot on submit/approve/reject, no dependencies), activation (snapshot used for financials and dependencies stored), and manual redetermination (snapshot at key lifecycle points, may store dependencies). Also notes that “Generating Payments” is listed as an active trigger, but the excerpt cuts off before details.

children.title: Reactive Determination Calculations
children.summary: Explains reactive determination recalculation in Cúram CER. After a case is activated, the engine stores dependencies for the initial assessment result so later data changes can trigger reassessment automatically. Changed inputs are written to a precedent change set, and deferred processing uses stored dependencies to recalculate affected dependents, including cases. Input changes may be to case, personal, rate, or product configuration data. The dependency manager is spreadsheet-like and does not interpret data types; it reassesses all dependent cases even if the final determination does not change.

This section explains when CER determination results are calculated. It contrasts active requests, where the Engine explicitly asks for a result, with reactive recalculation, where the Dependency Manager reassesses cases after relevant data changes. Active triggers include interim pre-activation “what if” requests, approval snapshots, activation snapshots that store dependencies and feed financials, manual redetermination, and payment generation. It also describes how activation records dependencies so later changes to case, personal, rate, or product configuration data can automatically trigger deferred reassessment.

children.title: How a Determination Result Is Calculated
children.summary: This section explains how a determination result is produced through a chain of CER rules. It distinguishes fixed rules (non-customizable, handling legislative periods and product-specific rule object creation) from product-specific rules (business/technical logic for eligibility, entitlement, explanations, and data links). It outlines the execution flow: calculate case lifetime, match overlapping product periods, create rule objects, compute results, and splice period results into one overall determination. It also notes that key decision factors are deprecated.

children.title: Example
children.summary: Explains how a determination result is calculated in Cúram Express Rules using fixed and product-specific rules, including lifetime and product period processing, rule object creation, data searches, and splicing results across periods. The example traces a Lone Parent Benefit case through evidence gathering, approval, activation, dependency storage, recalculation after life events, overpayment recovery, changing reasons for ineligibility, and eventual case closure, illustrating how historical and future determination outcomes are maintained.

Explains how CER determines case results over a product delivery case’s lifetime. Defines the “three Es” of a determination result: eligibility, entitlement, and explanation, including that entitlement applies only during eligible periods and explanations help case workers understand decisions. Describes case lifetime handling, including open-ended cases, past/future periods, and reassessment when start/end dates change. Covers active versus reactive calculation triggers, dependency storage after activation, and the rule chain used to calculate and splice period results into one overall determination, with a Lone Parent Benefit example.

children.title: Determination Storage
Example
children.summary: Explains how determination history is stored and used for financial processing. It describes two database table groups: CREOLECaseDetermination for full determination snapshots and CaseDecision for eligibility/entitlement outputs. An example shows a Lone Parent Benefit case changing over time, with records superseded as circumstances change. It also introduces the financial scheduler, which converts assessment determinations and case deductions into financial schedules for the Financial Manager, and notes that financial schedules are created only from assessment determinations.

Explains how the Engine stores active and reactive determinations as snapshots for later viewing and financial processing. It distinguishes two table groups: CREOLECaseDetermination for full determination history and CaseDecision for eligibility/entitlement results used in payment processing. An example shows superseded records as a Lone Parent Benefit case changes over time. Also describes the financial scheduler, which converts assessment determinations and case deductions into financial schedules for the Financial Manager; only assessment determinations generate financial schedules.

children.title: Scheduling Financials
Example
children.summary: This example shows how case determinations drive financial scheduling over time: changing entitlement rates create, cancel, and replace financial components; eligibility loss can trigger overpayment correction liabilities; and if no eligible decisions exist, no schedules are created. It also notes that closing a case cancels live financial components. The section then introduces determination retrieval, where case workers can view current, historical, approval-related, or manual determination snapshots, and the system analyzes change dates to split the case lifetime into coverage periods.

Explains how the financial scheduler converts assessment determination results and case deductions into financial components for the Financial Manager to process into payment or billing line items. Notes that schedules are created only from assessment determinations, not all determination types. This section mainly positions the scheduler between the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine and the Financial Manager, and ties scheduling to eligibility, entitlement, and deductions.

children.title: Determination Retrieval
Example
children.summary: This section gives an example of “coverage periods” in a determination, showing how eligibility, entitlement, and explanation changes split a case timeline into date ranges. It then introduces navigating determinations in the Engine, explaining that the system stores manual eligibility check, snapshot, and assessment determinations, and that users can drill into details such as eligibility/entitlement results and key decision factors.

This section explains determination retrieval in a product delivery case. It says case workers can view the current assessment determination, historical superseded determinations, approval/rejection snapshots, and manual determination results. When a snapshot is opened, the system analyzes eligibility, entitlement, and explanation changes to split the case timeline into coverage periods.

Overview of Eligibility and Entitlement processing in CER: configure a Product, gather input/evidence, calculate and store determinations, schedule financials, retrieve history, and reassess when circumstances or product rules change. Explains the “three Es” (eligibility, entitlement, explanation), product periods, Dependency Manager/precedent change sets for deferred recalculation, and how active/reactive determinations are stored and turned into financial components. Also covers case-worker retrieval of current and historical snapshots and splitting timelines into coverage periods.

children.title: 1.3 Navigating Determinations
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces how the Engine shows determinations as coverage periods with from/to dates, including open-ended periods and “vertical slice” views of decision data for a selected date. Gives an example of splitting a case lifetime into periods where eligibility, entitlement, and explanations change. Also begins “Navigating Determinations,” explaining that the Engine stores manual eligibility check, snapshot, and assessment determinations, and that users can drill into details such as eligibility/entitlement results and key decision factors.

children.title: Manual Check Determinations
children.summary: Explains determination types shown in Cúram Express Rules: manual check determinations, snapshot determinations, and assessment determinations. Manual eligibility checks can use in-edit evidence (treating pending evidence as applied) or active evidence only; the result shows whether in-edit evidence was used and the case worker can reopen the last one. Snapshot determinations are recorded on submission and approval/rejection. Assessment determinations are created by assess requests, drive financial processing, and are shown in Determination History and the Current Determination page.

children.title: Snapshot Determinations
children.summary: Describes snapshot determinations, which the Engine automatically records when a case is submitted for approval and when it is approved or rejected. These snapshots are shown on the Case Calendar, where users can also open a decision summary. The section also sits in context with manual eligibility checks and assessment determinations, but the core focus is on automatic recording of case determination results at key approval states.

children.title: Assessment Determinations
Current Assessment Determination
children.summary: Explains how the Engine shows the current assessment determination on the Determinations tab. This is the active result governing product delivery, typically the payable amount. If no case has been activated yet, no current assessment determination exists and a message is shown instead. Closely related nearby topics include manual check determinations, snapshot determinations, and the determination history.

children.title: Historical Assessment Determinations
children.summary: Explains how the Determinations tab shows assessment history for a case: the current determination appears first, followed by superseded historical determinations. Clarifies that each determination covers the full case lifetime and reflects the engine’s best prediction based on facts known at the time. Also describes manual reassessment, where a case worker can request an active case be reassessed and the engine records that the new result came from a manual request.

children.title: Manual Reassessments
children.summary: Explains manual reassessments in the Determination History area. A case worker can request a manual reassessment of an active case, must confirm the request, and the Engine then produces a new determination result marked as caused by the manual reassessment. The section also situates this within historical assessment determinations, where current determinations are shown above superseded ones.

Explains assessment determinations on the Determinations tab. The Engine creates an assessment determination for active or reactive case assessment requests, and the current one governs product delivery, typically the payable amount. If no case has been activated, no current assessment exists and a message is shown. Also notes the related Determination History list of current and superseded results, plus the option to manually force reassessment of an active case.

Explains how the Engine navigates determinations by coverage period, including open-ended periods and “vertical slice” decision views for a selected date. Covers the main determination types: manual eligibility checks, snapshot determinations, and assessment determinations. Manual checks can use in-edit or active evidence only and note whether pending evidence was included. Snapshots are recorded automatically on submission and approval/rejection and shown in the Case Calendar. Assessment determinations are created by assess requests, drive current product delivery/financial processing, and appear in Current Determination and Determination History, with reassessment options.

children.title: 1.4 Calculating and Displaying Eligibility and Entitlement
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the chapter on calculating and displaying eligibility and entitlement in Cúram 8.1.2. It explains historical assessment determinations in the Determinations tab, including current versus superseded determinations and how they reflect changing facts over a case’s lifetime. It also covers manual reassessments, where a case worker can request a new determination snapshot, such as for an appeal. The chapter frames eligibility/entitlement processing in reverse chronological order, focusing on how results are displayed and then calculated.

children.title: How It Looks
Viewing a Determination's Coverage Periods
children.summary: Explains how to view a determination’s coverage periods for a product delivery case. A determination spans the case’s full lifetime as known when made, is open-ended, and is divided into contiguous coverage periods representing constant eligibility, entitlement, and explanation. For each period, the system displays the eligibility result and a summary of the payable amount using standard Engine screens for CER-based cases. Notes that custom screens are usually unnecessary unless special requirements exist.

children.title: Basic Eligibility/entitlement Decision Details
children.summary: Explains how the Engine displays eligibility/entitlement results for CER-based cases, including standard summary screens, coverage periods, and how determinations span a case’s lifetime. Notes that eligibility/entitlement data is fixed by the Engine, used by Financials, and usually needs no custom screens. This section specifically introduces a very basic entitlement view that presents case entitlement information as a technical list of objectives and tags.

Explains how eligibility and entitlement results are displayed to case workers in CER-based cases. The Engine provides standard summary screens because the data structure is fixed and also supports Financials processing. It describes viewing a determination’s coverage periods: a determination spans the case’s full known lifetime, is open-ended, and is split into contiguous periods of constant eligibility/entitlement/explanation, each showing the eligibility result and payable amount. Also introduces a very basic entitlement details view listing objectives and tags.

children.title: How It Works
Calculation of Eligibility and Entitlement
(deprecated) A) ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet ProductDeliveryCase rule object
children.summary: Deprecated fixed Engine rule object for eligibility/entitlement calculation. Describes how ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase is auto-created in memory with case data (case ID, dates) when a determination is triggered, and that its behavior is not customizable. Its calculated attributes are fixed: contributingProductPeriods comes from the Engine’s Statics.contributingProductPeriods and determinationResult from Statics.determinationResult. Useful for understanding the top-level, non-customizable layer of determination result processing.

children.title: B) ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductPeriod rule objects
children.summary: Defines how the Engine creates in-memory ProductPeriod rule objects from database product period records, one per published period, with start and end dates. These objects are used as part of the fixed eligibility/entitlement calculation flow described in the surrounding section, especially for contributing product periods and determination results. For a new product, there is typically only one period covering the product’s lifetime.

children.title: C) ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet AbstractCase rule class
children.summary: Introduces the ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.AbstractCase interface rule class, which the Engine creates for each Product Period to bridge fixed eligibility/entitlement processing with product-specific logic. It requires implementations for isEligibleTimeline and objectiveTimelines, covering eligibility intervals, entitlement to objectives, and tag values. The section also explains that custom product period rule classes must extend this interface, preferably via DefaultProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.DefaultCase for upgrade ease, and that these implementations carry most of the eligibility/entitlement business logic.

children.title: D) Custom rule classes for eligibility/entitlement
children.summary: Defines how custom eligibility/entitlement rule classes are plugged into a product period. The Engine selects the rule class configured on the product period, and it must extend ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.AbstractCase, preferably DefaultProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.DefaultCase for upgrade safety. The custom class must implement isEligibleTimeline and objectiveTimelines, which drive the product’s eligibility and entitlement calculations. This section is about the required inheritance, default base class recommendation, and the two core rule attributes developers must provide.

children.title: E) Custom rule classes for calculations
children.summary: Explains how to structure complex eligibility/entitlement calculations using custom CER rule classes in `isEligibleTimeline` and `objectiveTimelines`. These classes need not implement application interfaces, though CER `extends` can be used for polymorphism. Notes that calculations may depend on stored rate cells, custom entities, and custom evidence, and that these accesses create dependencies used for reassessment when underlying data changes.

children.title: F) RateRuleSet.RateCell rule objects and propagation configuration
children.summary: Explains RateRuleSet.RateCell rule objects, which the Engine stores on CER database tables for configured rate tables. When rate table cells change, their values are propagated into a valueTimeline rule attribute, with the set of propagated tables controlled by configuration and not customizable beyond that. These rate cells are used in eligibility/entitlement calculations for comparisons and multiplications by rates. Also notes that stored-data retrieval creates dependencies used for reassessment when underlying data changes.

children.title: G) Custom Entity rule objects
children.summary: Explains how CER custom entity rule objects are populated during determination calculations. When a determination starts, the Engine checks rule object data configurations to find entities whose data is needed for rules processing, then loads corresponding CER rule objects into memory. Population is handled by a generic Engine mechanism. Useful for understanding how custom stored entity data becomes available to eligibility/entitlement rules and where to look for the broader Entity Rule Objects model.

children.title: H) Custom Evidence rule objects
children.summary: Explains how CER custom evidence rule objects are used in eligibility/entitlement calculations. When a determination is triggered, the Engine checks rule object data configurations to identify relevant static or dynamic evidence types, then populates CER rule objects in memory using a generic mechanism. These objects may be retrieved by custom calculation rule classes alongside rate cells and custom entities, and their stored data creates dependencies used for reassessment when evidence changes.

children.title: I) DeterminationResult
children.summary: Defines the engine-supplied, immutable `DeterminationResult` for `ProductDeliveryCase.determinationResult`. It is created by splicing eligibility/entitlement results across product periods and stored in the database. Key fields include `productDeliveryID`, `determinationDateRange`, and `determinationEligibilityEntitlementTimeline`, plus other non-eligibility data. The structure cannot be customized. Also notes display-time processing: when viewed, the engine divides the determination into coverage periods and shows an entitlement summary for each.

Explains the eligibility/entitlement calculation flow within a determination result. Covers fixed Engine layers that create ProductDeliveryCase and ProductPeriod rule objects and build immutable DeterminationResult, plus the extension point where each product period uses a custom AbstractCase rule class. Describes required rule attributes isEligibleTimeline and objectiveTimelines, the recommendation to extend DefaultCase, and how custom calculations may use rate cells, custom entities, and custom evidence, with dependencies recorded for reassessment.

children.title: Display of Eligibility and Entitlement
Dividing the Determination into Coverage Periods
children.summary: Explains how the Engine automatically splits a determination’s lifetime into contiguous coverage periods whenever eligibility, entitlement, or decision details change. Notes that changes in key decision factors alone do not start a new period, and the split behavior on standard viewing screens cannot be customized. Also describes the optional DeterminationIntervalSummarizerStrategy interface for displaying a localized summary of entitlement per coverage period, with no effect on case reassessment or financial output if changed or omitted.

children.title: Displaying a Summary of Entitlement for a Coverage Period
children.summary: Explains how the Engine displays a summary of entitlement for each coverage period in a determination. It describes the `DeterminationIntervalSummarizerStrateg` interface, which can be implemented per product to generate a localized summary line when coverage periods are shown. If no implementation is configured, no summary appears. Changing the implementation only affects display output, not case reassessment or financial results, so this setup is optional and can be deferred.

Explains display-time handling of eligibility and entitlement. When a determination is viewed, the Engine automatically splits the case lifetime into contiguous coverage periods wherever eligibility, entitlement, or decision details change; key decision factor changes alone do not start a new period, and this behavior on standard screens cannot be customized. It also describes the optional `DeterminationIntervalSummarizerStrateg` interface for showing a localized entitlement summary per coverage period. If not configured, no summary appears; changing it affects only display, not reassessment or financial results.

Explains how eligibility/entitlement is calculated and displayed. Calculation is split between fixed Engine processing and product-specific CER rules: ProductDeliveryCase/ProductPeriod are Engine-managed, while each period uses a custom AbstractCase-derived rule class implementing isEligibleTimeline and objectiveTimelines, typically extending DefaultCase. Custom calculations may use rate cells, entities, and evidence, with dependencies recorded for reassessment. At view time, the Engine splits determinations into contiguous coverage periods when eligibility, entitlement, or decision details change, and can show a localized entitlement summary via DeterminationIntervalSummarizerStrateg.

children.title: How to Use It
Understanding Eligibility and Entitlement Concepts
Case Lifetime
children.summary: Defines case lifetime as the inclusive period between a case’s start and end dates, using actual dates when present, otherwise expected dates; cases with no end date are open-ended. Explains that eligibility is a Boolean that can vary across the case timeline, and gives child benefit as an example of eligibility ending when a child reaches majority. Also introduces objectives as the deliverables of a product delivery case, such as payments, billing amounts, or non-monetary outcomes.

children.title: Eligibility
children.summary: Introduces the eligibility/entitlement section and explains that most high-level processing is fixed by the Engine, but product-specific lower-level logic must be implemented through analysis, implementation, and testing. Defines core concepts: case lifetime (actual vs expected start/end dates, open-ended cases), eligibility as a Boolean timeline over the case lifetime, and objectives as what a delivery case provides to a target (e.g., benefit, bill, or recommendation).

children.title: Objectives
children.summary: Defines the concept of an objective in eligibility/entitlement processing: something “delivered” by a product delivery case to a target, typically a benefit payment but also potentially a bill amount or non-monetary outcome like a recommendation. The section sits in the context of the Engine’s fixed eligibility/entitlement data model and explains that implementers must map business requirements to these concepts. Open this node if you need the core meaning of objectives within the case lifetime and eligibility framework.

children.title: Objective Tags
children.summary: Explains “objective tags” as delivery frequencies for objectives, such as daily or weekly payment options. Distinguishes tag types defined by the product’s objective type from tag instances on a specific objective instance, including examples like paying $10 per week to a claimant. Notes that objective tags support mixed delivery frequencies and ramp-up/ramp-down payment periods. The section also begins analysis guidance for mapping eligibility/entitlement requirements to engine concepts, including checking product periods and legislation changes.

Explains how eligibility and entitlement logic is structured in the Engine and what implementers must do: analysis, implementation, and testing of lower-level product-specific logic. Defines core concepts: case lifetime as the inclusive period between start and end dates (actual dates override expected; no end date means open-ended), eligibility as a Boolean timeline over that lifetime, and objectives as what a product delivery case delivers to a target, such as payments, billing amounts, or non-monetary recommendations.

children.title: Analysis
Identify the product periods for your product
children.summary: Explains how to identify product periods for a product by checking whether legislation changes require multiple product periods. New products usually start with a single product period because they typically have one legislation version; later legislative changes may add more periods. This sits within broader guidance on mapping eligibility/entitlement requirements to engine concepts, after distinguishing objective types/instances and objective tag types/instances.

children.title: Identify what types of objective are delivered by your product, and at what frequencies
children.summary: This section explains how to design product objectives for each product period. It says to identify what the product actually delivers (for example benefit payments, multiple financial components, or recommendations), map those deliveries to objective types, and decide whether to use fewer complex objectives or more simple ones based on nominee flexibility and aggregation rules. It also covers choosing delivery frequencies for each objective type—typically daily, with optional weekly or monthly rates—so ramp-up/ramp-down and larger-unit eligibility can be handled.

children.title: Identify the rules that govern when a case is eligible
children.summary: Explains how to determine case eligibility rules in CER: eligibility may depend on claimant/household categories and can be independent of, or intertwined with, entitlement objectives. Eligibility is calculated as a time-based Boolean timeline, so it may vary by day and be affected by events on other days. Also introduces objective creation patterns for cases: a single objective per type or multiple objectives per qualifying member, with examples like household and child allowances.

children.title: Identify the rules that govern the objectives for each case
children.summary: Explains how to determine objective instances for a product delivery case. It highlights two common patterns: a single objective instance per case, or multiple objective instances created per eligible case member. Example: a household may receive one basic allowance plus one child allowance per child. Notes that the engine does not enforce these patterns, so objective creation can be as complex as business requirements require.

children.title: Identify the rules that determine when an objective has been attained and its target
children.summary: Explains how to determine when an objective is attained and its target over a case’s lifetime, including entitlement that can change over time and may be simple or complex. Also covers analyzing the values at which an objective can be delivered for each frequency, including fixed rates, rate-table lookups, and calculations that may derive one frequency from another. Ends with implementation guidance: editing or creating eligibility/entitlement rule sets in the dynamic product wizard, including the default rule-set naming convention.

children.title: Identify the rules that determine the values at which an objective can be delivered
children.summary: Explains how to determine entitlement and delivery values for objectives. It says to analyze when a case is entitled to an objective over time, the target and reference data for each objective instance, and the values used when delivered. Values may be fixed rates, rate-table values, or complex calculations varying by frequency (e.g., daily vs weekly vs monthly). Notes that the Engine allows but does not require cross-frequency patterns. Ends with implementation guidance: a default eligibility/entitlement rule set is created by the dynamic product wizard and can be edited or renamed.

Guidance for mapping product requirements to CER eligibility/entitlement concepts. It covers identifying product periods, usually starting with one for new products but adding more when legislation changes require it. It explains designing objective types and delivery frequencies (often daily, sometimes weekly/monthly), then analyzing case eligibility rules as a time-based boolean timeline that may depend on claimant/household categories and can interact with entitlement. It also covers choosing single vs multiple objective instances per case member.

children.title: Implementation
Write the Product Structure Rule Classes
children.summary: Explains how to define product structure rule classes for CER eligibility/entitlement. Covers the required hierarchy: one Product rule class, one or more Objective Type classes, and one or more Objective Tag Type classes, usually extending default rule classes for upgrades. Describes inherited attributes (objectiveTypes, tagTypes, IDs, names, codes, patterns, etc.), naming guidance, and how to wire classes together using fixed lists of create expressions. Includes examples for default dynamic product wizard rule sets and notes to edit defaults to match product requirements.

children.title: Write the Case Eligibility/Entitlement Calculation Rule Classes
children.summary: Explains how to implement eligibility/entitlement calculation rule classes for each product period: a case rule, objective instance rules, and objective tag instance rules. Recommends extending default classes from the wizard, editing default implementations, and factoring shared logic. Covers key inherited attributes and how to implement them: case isEligibleTimeline and objectiveTimelines; objective isEntitledTimeline, targetIDTimeline, relatedReferenceTimeline, description, and tagTimelines; tag valueTimeline and tagType. Also describes creating objective instances from the case and tag instances from objectives using fixed or dynamic lists, with optional context attributes passed via create expressions.

children.title: Write the Product Periods
children.summary: Explains how to create product periods for CER-based products and link each period to the product structure and eligibility/entitlement rule classes. Covers two deployment paths: in development, add DMX records for CREOLERuleClassLink and CREOLEProductPeriod; in a running system, publish rule sets and configure product periods in the admin UI. Notes each product period needs separate rule class link records, even if reused, and key decision factors/display categories are not required for eligibility/entitlement rules.

children.title: Choose or Create a Summarizer Strategy
children.summary: Explains how to configure a product to use a determination interval summarizer strategy for displaying entitlement summaries. You can set the strategy either in development via the CREOLEProduct.dmx detIntSummarizerStrategyType column, or in a running system through Product Delivery Cases > Rule Sets > Eligibility Determination. It lists built-in strategies (blank, total daily, weekly, monthly monetary entitlement) and notes that custom strategies can be created by adding a code table entry, implementing DeterminationIntervalSummarizerStrategy, and binding it in a Guice module.

Explains how to implement CER eligibility/entitlement for a benefit product. Covers creating and wiring product structure, objective type, and objective tag type rule classes; extending default rule classes; and using timelines, fixed/dynamic lists, and create expressions to build case, objective, and tag instances. Also describes linking product periods to rule classes in DMX or the admin UI, and configuring an entitlement summarizer strategy via DMX/UI or custom implementation.

children.title: Testing
children.summary: Guidance for testing CER eligibility/entitlement rules and custom determination interval summarizer strategies. Recommends unit tests for individual rules, end-to-end tests for evidence and product delivery case scenarios, and manual testing of online flows. Also warns that every case lifetime must be fully covered by exactly one product period; otherwise the engine records DeterminationProblem entries, logs the issue, and shows “Eligibility could not be determined” for affected coverage periods.

Overview of how to implement CER eligibility/entitlement for a product. Explains core concepts: case lifetime, eligibility as a Boolean timeline, objectives and objective tags. Guides analysis of product periods, objective types/frequencies, eligibility rules, and objective instance patterns. Shows implementation via rule classes, timelines, DMX/admin configuration, and summarizer strategies. Includes testing advice and warns that every case lifetime must be covered by exactly one product period, or the engine logs DeterminationProblems and shows “Eligibility could not be determined.”

Explains how Cúram 8.1.2 calculates and displays eligibility/entitlement for CER-based cases. Covers historical determinations and manual reassessments, the reverse-chronological flow from results to rules, and standard coverage-period and basic entitlement-detail views. Describes Engine vs product-specific responsibilities: product periods, rule classes, timelines, determination storage, and summarizer strategies. Also includes implementation steps for product periods and display strategies, plus testing guidance and the error behavior when no product period covers a case lifetime.

Explains that the Engine can output fixed-structure, display-only data showing what influenced eligibility/entitlement decisions, without affecting processing or payments. Covers how factors appear in graphical and list views, how the Engine splices product-period data and stores it in determinations, and how custom CER rule classes implement keyDataTimelines and optional keyEvents. Also includes product-period linking, deployment in development/running systems, testing, and runtime error handling.

children.title: 1.6 Calculating and Displaying Decision Details
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces decision details in Cúram 8.1.2: the Engine can calculate and display product-specific eligibility/entitlement breakdowns on free-form UIM screens. It notes key decision factors are deprecated and emphasizes these details are display-only, not used in financials or processing. The chapter explains the flow in reverse order to help designers start from the desired screen layout and navigation, and is organized into “How it looks,” “How it works,” and “How to use it.”

children.title: How It Looks
Multi-locale support for CER
children.summary: Explains how CER decision details are presented to caseworkers, emphasizing that the data structure is highly flexible and requires custom dynamic UIM screens. Introduces multi-locale support via the curam.display.rules.multi.locale application property, with references to related configuration documentation. Also covers summary display categories for coverage periods, including automatically supplied headers, tabbed display categories, recommended overall summary tab behavior, and decision comparison features that show changes from previous case decisions.

children.title: Summary Display Category
children.summary: Describes the Summary Display Category for decision details in Cúram Express Rules. It explains how a coverage period’s decision details tab is presented: an automatically supplied header with case and coverage dates, a row of tabs for configured display categories, and a main summary area built with standard UIM components. Recommends making the first tab an overall eligibility/entitlement summary because it is shown by default and in expanded determinations. Also notes multi-locale support for decision details and a decision comparison feature showing prior case decisions.

children.title: Decision Comparison
children.summary: Explains how decision details are displayed to caseworkers using dynamic UIM screens, since decision details are flexible and not automatically renderable by the Engine. Notes multi-locale support and configurable localizable display rules. Describes the summary display category for coverage periods, including the engine-supplied header, tabbed display categories, and recommendation that the first tab be an overall eligibility/entitlement summary. Also introduces decision comparison, where a decision details page can show data from the previous case decision to highlight changes over time.

children.title: Sub-screens
children.summary: Explains Cúram decision-details “sub-screens” that let rows on a decision details page expand to show more detail for case worker drill-down. Introduces basic eligibility/entitlement screens as simple, mostly example output showing overall eligibility and entitled objectives/tags, intended for early reuse only. Describes how fixed Engine processing and custom product-specific rules combine to display decision details, notes display categories are product-level, and that some product periods may omit rules and show “no details available.” Includes an income/asset legislation change example.

children.title: Basic Eligibility/entitlement Information
children.summary: Introduces the Engine’s basic eligibility/entitlement screens and decision detail rules, which display overall case eligibility and, if eligible, objectives/tags. Notes these outputs are intentionally limited and mainly useful as temporary starter examples or templates for building custom decision details. Also begins the “How It Works” explanation of how fixed Engine processing combines with custom product-specific logic, and mentions display categories are configured at product level, with optional decision detail rules per product period.

Explains how CER decision details are presented to caseworkers. Because decision details are highly flexible, the Engine cannot render them automatically, so designers must build dynamic UIM screens. Covers multi-locale support via the curam.display.rules.multi.locale property, the engine-supplied summary display category for coverage periods (automatic header, product-configured tab strip, main summary area), recommended default first tab for overall eligibility/entitlement, and decision comparison showing prior case decision data. Mentions the deprecated key decision factors feature.

children.title: How It Works
Calculation of Decision Details
A) ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase rule object
children.summary: This section identifies the fixed ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase rule object as the single rule object controlling overall case determination for decision details, mirroring the deprecated eligibility/entitlement pattern. It also introduces the ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet AbstractCase rule class as the interface between Engine-provided fixed processing and product-specific rules for calculating decision details for the top-level display category screen. The broader section explains how decision details are calculated and displayed via layered fixed and custom implementations.

children.title: B) ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet AbstractCase rule class
children.summary: Defines the AbstractCase rule class as the Engine’s fixed interface to product-specific decision details calculation for a case. It supports decision details for a top-level display category screen, while sub-screen handling is covered later. The section sits within the broader decision details architecture, which separates fixed Engine processing from custom product implementations and produces in-memory rule objects used to build the determination result’s decision details.

children.title: C) Custom rule classes for decision details
children.summary: Explains how to create custom rule classes for decision details and sub-screen details in Cúram Express Rules. The Engine selects a rule class per product period/display category, and it must extend ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCase, preferably via DefaultProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.DefaultCase. Sub-screen data uses ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCaseSubscreenDisplay, preferably DefaultCaseSubscreenDisplay, with businessObjectID as a required attribute. Notes that Display and DisplaySubscreen attributes carry most implementation work and may reuse or refactor eligibility/entitlement calculation rules.

children.title: D) ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCaseSubscreenDisplay rule class
children.summary: Explains the AbstractCaseSubscreenDisplay rule class for decision details sub-screens. It is the interface between Engine-managed decision detail processing and product-specific logic for expanded rows in dynamic UI screens. Concrete rule classes must ultimately extend this class, preferably via DefaultProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.DefaultCaseSubscreenDisplay, and implement the required businessObjectID attribute to identify the expanded object. Also notes that sub-screen data is typically provided through attributes annotated with Display, often reusing existing calculation rules.

children.title: E) Custom rule classes for sub-screen details
children.summary: This section explains custom rule classes for sub-screen decision details in Cúram Express Rules. It says sub-screen data for expanded rows in dynamic UIM screens must use a rule class that ultimately extends ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCaseSubscreenDisplay, with DefaultProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.DefaultCaseSubscreenDisplay recommended for upgrades. The section notes that these classes typically implement attributes annotated with Display, and may reuse or adapt calculation rules used for eligibility/entitlement logic.

children.title: F) Custom rule classes for calculations
children.summary: This section says decision-details and sub-screen data may need complex business recalculation, so custom “calculator” rule classes can be used. It notes that Display and DisplaySubscreen attributes typically reuse existing eligibility/entitlement calculation rules, though some refactoring may be needed so shared rules work for both purposes.

children.title: G) DeterminationResult
children.summary: This section explains how DeterminationResult stores decision details in determinationDecisionDetailsTimelines, a map from each display category to a timeline of XML data. It describes how each product period contributes XML for a category only if rules exist, and how missing rules produce empty XML and no decision details. It also outlines the engine’s recursive process for finding change dates and generating XML by walking Display-annotated timelines, nested rule objects, and lists, with special handling for sub-screen data and business object IDs.

children.title: Basic Eligibility/Entitlement example XML output
children.summary: Shows an example XML output for basic eligibility/entitlement decision details produced by the Engine’s `AbstractBasicProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractBasicCase` rule class. It illustrates the `DecisionDetails` root, `BasicCase` element, eligibility timeline, objective timelines, and subscreen/tag data, with annotated domain types and values. The section explains the XML structure and how point-in-time rule object attributes are recorded for a coverage period.

Explains how decision details are calculated and stored in DeterminationResult, using a layered model of fixed Engine processing and custom product rules. It introduces the fixed ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase controller, the ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCase interface for top-level display categories, and AbstractCaseSubscreenDisplay for expanded sub-screens. Custom rule classes extend these interfaces and use Display/DisplaySubscreen attributes, often reusing eligibility/entitlement calculation rules. It also describes how the Engine walks timelines, rule objects, and lists to build XML timelines per display category.

children.title: Display of Decision Details
Basic Eligibility/Entitlement UIM examples
children.summary: Explains Cúram decision-details UIM examples for basic eligibility/entitlement. Shows how a Dynamic UIM page uses server interfaces to fetch XML, XPath-like expressions to read it, and conditions/lists to render content. Demonstrates a Boolean condition for eligible vs ineligible clusters, a list of entitlement objectives using Item[] paths, and linking a top-level screen to an inline sub-screen via PAGE parameters and businessObjectID. References CREOLEDisplayRules_basicCaseDisplay and CREOLEDisplayRules_basicCaseDisplay_objectiveTagSubscreen.

Explains how decision details are displayed in Cúram for coverage periods. Describes the Engine flow: choose a Dynamic UIM page by display category, call a server interface to fetch XML, and use XPath-like expressions in the page to populate fields, conditions, lists, and sub-screens. Covers supported interfaces for top-level, subscreen, previous-period, and comparison data. Includes basic eligibility/entitlement examples showing a Boolean eligibility condition, a list of entitlement objectives, and an inline sub-screen linked via PAGE parameters and businessObjectID.

Explains how decision details are calculated and displayed for determinations. Covers the split between fixed Engine processing and custom product-specific CER rules, including top-level display categories, sub-screens, and timeline/XML generation stored in DeterminationResult. Also describes runtime display flow: Dynamic UIM page selection by display category, server interfaces to fetch XML, XPath-like data binding, and expandable rows/sub-screens via PAGE parameters and businessObjectID. Includes basic eligibility/entitlement examples for condition, list, and inline sub-screen rendering.

children.title: How to Use It
Understanding Decision Details Concepts
children.summary: Defines the core decision details concepts in Cúram Engine: top-level screens, sub-screens, case rule classes, display attributes, DisplaySubscreen attributes, and businessObjectID. It explains that decision details requirements should be analyzed against these concepts before implementation, noting they are usually based on case-worker usefulness rather than legislation. It also warns that key decision factors are deprecated and suggests revisiting requirements after the product is live, since decision details can be changed independently of eligibility/entitlement calculations.

children.title: Analysis
Identify the Display Categories
children.summary: This section explains how to design display categories for eligibility/entitlement decision screens. It advises naming categories and choosing their order carefully, since the first category is the default tab and the default expanded coverage-period view, ideally showing overall summary details. It also covers sketching screen layouts, including expandable sub-screens, conditional data, and special formatting; mapping each displayed item to source or transformed eligibility/entitlement data; identifying numeric keys for expandable rows; and considering comparison data from the previous case decision.

children.title: Sketch out the Screens
children.summary: Explains how to sketch decision-detail screens for eligibility/entitlement. Designers should define display categories and order, with the first tab serving as the default summary and expanded coverage-period view. For each category, sketch realistic layouts, including expandable sub-screens, conditional content, and non-standard formatting. Map every displayed item to its eligibility/entitlement source, noting any screen-specific derived values. For expandable sub-screens, identify a unique numerical businessObjectID key. Also consider showing comparison data from the previous case decision.

children.title: Map displayed data to eligibility/entitlement data
children.summary: Explains how to map eligibility/entitlement rule data to what appears on decision details screens. Covers choosing and ordering display categories, sketching screen layouts, identifying which displayed values come directly from rules versus requiring screen-specific calculations, assigning businessObjectID keys for expandable sub-screens, and planning comparison data from prior case decisions. Uses a “means test” example to show when display values may need extra transformation beyond the underlying rule logic.

children.title: Identify keys for sub-screens
children.summary: For expandable sub-screens, assign a unique numerical businessObjectID to identify the expanded row and the specific sub-screen to display. The section also explains how to design display categories, sketch screens for data layout and conditional/non-standard elements, map each displayed item to eligibility/entitlement data or screen-specific calculations, and identify comparison data from prior case decisions.

children.title: Identify comparison data
children.summary: Explains how to prepare decision-detail screens for eligibility/entitlement: identify and order display categories, sketch screen layouts with any expandable sub-screens and conditional/non-standard content, and map each displayed item to its source in eligibility/entitlement data or screen-specific calculations. Also notes the need for unique numerical keys for expandable sub-screens. This section introduces comparison data, which can let a top-level screen show information from the previous case decision in addition to coverage-period data from the current determination.

Explains how to analyze decision-details requirements before implementation. You should define and order display categories, with the first tab/expanded view serving as a summary screen. Sketch each category’s layout, including expandable sub-screens, conditional content, and special formatting. Map every displayed item to eligibility/entitlement data or screen-specific transformations, such as derived “means test” status. For expandable rows, assign a unique numerical businessObjectID. Also consider whether top-level screens should include comparison data from the previous case decision.

children.title: Implementation
Write the Case rule class
children.summary: Explains how to implement the case-level decision details rule class for a display category. The rule must extend ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCase, with DefaultProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.DefaultCase recommended for easier upgrades. It notes that decision details can often reuse eligibility/entitlement calculations, may be split across rule sets, and can include supporting intermediate rule classes. It also frames the need for one rule class and one dynamic UIM page per product period/display category, and mentions that some data may compare against the previous case decision period.

children.title: Implement attributes to return top-level screen data
children.summary: Explains how to extend `DefaultProductKeyDataRuleSet.DefaultCase` with Display attributes to expose top-level screen data for decision details. Recommends using existing eligibility/entitlement calculations via `<create>` and `<reference>`, keeping reusable rule objects in non-Display attributes, and implementing display-specific calculations only where needed. Also notes using `<dynamiclist>` for lists by wrapping items in rule objects with display annotations. Example use cases include claimant name, household count, and assets for a summary display category.

children.title: Implement attributes and rule classes for sub-screen data
children.summary: Explains how to implement rule attributes and rule classes for Dynamic UIM sub-screen data. For expandable rows, the case rule class must expose a list of rule objects via a DisplaySubscreen attribute, typically named Subscreens, so the Engine can search data when rows are expanded. The returned objects must extend AbstractCaseSubscreenDisplay, preferably via DefaultCaseSubscreenDisplay, and provide a unique businessObjectID. Example pattern shows wrapping eligibility/entitlement objects (e.g., assets and valuations) in sub-screen display classes, including nested drill-down support.

children.title: Write the Dynamic UIM screens
children.summary: Explains how to write Dynamic UIM screens for display rules.
