Top-level screens must accept determinationID and displayDate, call the CaseDetermination DISPLAY interface viewDecisionDisplayRulesCategoryXML, connect category ref from .properties, optionally retrieve previous-period XML, and render data via XPath-like queries. If rows expand, connect to a sub-screen. Sub-screens accept determinationID, displayDate, and businessObjectID, and call viewDecisionDisplayRuleCategorySubscreenXML with matching parameter connections. Includes a note to define CategoryRef and references an example UIM.

children.title: Configure the Product
children.summary: Explains how to configure a CER product to support decision-details display categories. In development, create DMX records for LocalizableText, TextTranslation, CREOLEProductDecisionDispCat, CREOLERuleClassLink, and CREOLEProductPeriodDispCat, with unique custom IDs and links to product periods, rule sets, rule classes, and dynamic UIM pages. In a running system, publish rule sets, then use the admin app to create display categories, set name/order/page/category reference, associate decision-details rules to product periods, and publish changes.

Implementation guidance for decision-details display categories. Covers creating case rule classes (prefer DefaultProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.DefaultCase), reusing eligibility/entitlement calculations, adding Display attributes for top-level data, and using DisplaySubscreen plus sub-screen display classes for expandable rows. Describes Dynamic UIM top-level and sub-screen pages, including parameters, server interfaces, category references, and XPath-based rendering. Also explains product configuration in development DMX files or a running system admin app, linking display categories to product periods, rule classes, and UIM pages.

children.title: Testing
children.summary: Testing guidance for decision details in Cúram 8.1.2. Recommends CER unit tests for rule logic, end-to-end tests for evidence and product delivery case scenarios, and manual UI testing because decision details use custom screens. Highlights verifying Dynamic UIM XPath-like expressions against XML returned by the Engine and using breakpoints on CaseDetermination methods to inspect XML. Notes extraneous XML data can affect coverage period splitting. Also covers runtime CER errors (e.g., missing references, division by zero), log/stack trace analysis, and debugging/retesting rules.

Explains how to build decision details for Cúram eligibility/entitlement products. Covers core concepts (top-level screens, sub-screens, case rule classes, Display and DisplaySubscreen attributes, businessObjectID), then analysis steps for display categories, screen layout, data mapping, sub-screen keys, and optional comparison data. Describes implementation: separate rule sets, Dynamic UIM pages, server interfaces, XPath-based rendering, and product configuration via DMX or the admin app. Ends with testing guidance: CER unit tests, end-to-end/manual UI tests, XML/XPath debugging, and handling runtime CER errors.

Explains how Cúram 8.1.2 calculates and displays decision details for eligibility/entitlement products on custom dynamic UIM screens. Covers the display-only nature of decision details, deprecated key decision factors, multi-locale support, summary tabs, decision comparison, and expandable sub-screens. Describes the split between Engine processing and product-specific CER rules, how results are stored in DeterminationResult and rendered via XML/XPath-like bindings, and how to implement/configure display categories and product periods using DMX or the admin app. Includes testing and runtime troubleshooting guidance.

children.title: 1.7 Understanding Rule Object Converters and Propagators
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces how Cúram eligibility and entitlement uses CER rule object converters to retrieve business data and turn it into rule objects, and rule object propagators to notify the Dependency Manager when data changes. Explains that converters and propagators usually come in matching pairs and may share configuration for selecting business data. Sets up an “Initial Assessment Example” tracing data conversion through product, person, case, member, income, and case activation, with reassessment/propagation covered later.

children.title: An Initial Assessment Example
A System Administrator Creates and Publishes Rule Set Information for a Product
children.summary: Explains how a system administrator creates a Product, associates and publishes its rule set information, and how publication populates key database tables used later in eligibility/entitlement calculations (CREOLEProductPeriod, CREOLEProductPeriodDispCat, CREOLERuleClassLink). Notes these tables are converted/propagated by the Entity converter and must not be altered. Also introduces creating a new effective-dated rate table (“Income Limits”) for a Sickness Benefit to store changing household income thresholds.

children.title: A System Administrator Creates and Publishes a New Rate Table
children.summary: This section explains how a system administrator creates and publishes a new rate table for a Sickness Benefit rule set. It introduces an “Income Limits” rate table used to compare household income against a time-varying eligibility threshold, including an effective-dated increase from this year’s limit to next year’s limit. It also situates the rate table within the broader process of publishing product rule set information required for eligibility and entitlement processing.

children.title: A Case Worker Registers a Person
children.summary: Explains a worked example of CER processing when a case worker registers a person and then a new case. It shows a person being added to the Person table, the Entity Rule Object Propagator notifying the Dependency Manager, and no further impact on calculations. It then describes creating a Sickness Benefit case, writing CaseHeader, ProductDelivery, and CaseParticipantRole rows, and how these are later converted into rule objects for eligibility and entitlement determination.

children.title: A Case Worker Creates a New Case for that Person
children.summary: After a person is registered, the case worker creates a Sickness Benefit case for him. The system writes CaseHeader and ProductDelivery rows, then later converts them to a ProductDeliveryCase rule object for case determination. It also records the father as a case member in CaseParticipantRole, later converted to a CaseParticipantRole rule object. The section highlights how case registration stores product and case-specific data in application tables for later CER rule-object conversion and processing.

children.title: A Case Worker Adds an Additional Member to the Case
children.summary: A case worker registers the father’s daughter as an additional case member, creating new Person and CaseParticipantRole records for later rule conversion. The worker also captures the father’s income history as evidence, with multiple dated records in one succession set. While in edit, the evidence is not used for calculations; after activation, active evidence is converted into a single income rule object with static and timeline values for eligibility and entitlement processing.

children.title: A Case Worker Captures and Activates Some Income Evidence
children.summary: Explains how a case worker records a father’s changing income as evidence for a Sickness Benefit case, then activates that evidence so it becomes “best known truth” for later eligibility/entitlement calculations. The section describes succession sets, in-edit versus active evidence, and how the Active Succession Set Rule Object Converter turns active income evidence into a single rule object with static and timeline values. It also notes that the case is then progressed toward approval and activation.

children.title: A Case Worker Activates the Case
children.summary: This section explains how a case worker prepares a case for determination by adding a household member and capturing/activating evidence, especially income evidence. It describes evidence succession sets, in-edit versus active evidence, and how active evidence is converted into rule objects by the Active Succession Set Rule Object Converter. It then introduces activating the case after members and evidence are recorded, which triggers case assessment for eligibility and entitlement processing.

Introduces how Cúram eligibility/entitlement uses rule object converters and propagators to turn business data into CER rule objects and notify the Dependency Manager of changes. The example traces a Sickness Benefit case lifecycle: publishing product rule set data, creating an effective-dated “Income Limits” rate table, registering a person and case, adding a household member, capturing/activating income evidence, and finally activating the case. It highlights key tables, converters, active evidence succession sets, and how data becomes “best known truth” for determination.

children.title: The Framework for Converters and Propagators
children.summary: This section explains the application framework for registering and invoking converters and propagators at runtime. It describes how components contribute to rule object conversion and propagation, and notes that each data type is typically managed by a converter/propagator pair with its own configuration. It also distinguishes fixed-behavior implementations from configurable ones, and situates this framework in the broader CER process of converting case data into rule objects used for eligibility/entitlement calculations.

children.title: Rule Objects for Use with Eligibility and Entitlement Processing
Product Delivery Rule Objects
Overview
children.summary: Explains rule object converters and propagators used for eligibility/entitlement processing in Cúram 8.1.2. Notes that each rule class can be mapped only once per converter/propagator and should not be reused across different implementations. Lists supported rule object types (Product Delivery, Rate, Entity, Active Succession Set, Active Evidence Row) and mentions other non-relevant application-specific converters/propagators are excluded. Introduces the section topics: rule objects, data configuration problems, data access points, logging, and supported domain types, then starts with Product Delivery rule objects.

children.title: Configuration
children.summary: Explains how CER-based product delivery cases are configured and processed. The propagator behavior is controlled by the “Reassessment Strategy” on each CER-based product. It describes conversion of a case into a ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase rule object, including fields populated from CaseHeader and ProductDelivery data, and how dependency tracking records date-based inputs for recalculation. It also notes that accessing Product Delivery rule objects does not create precedents, and outlines propagation when CaseHeader or ProductDelivery data changes, including the “Do not reassess closed cases” strategy.

children.title: Conversion Processing
children.summary: Explains how CER-based product delivery case data is converted and used during assessment and propagation. The case is mapped to a ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase rule object, with a determinationResult attribute calculated on demand from case header fields such as caseID, concernRoleID, dates, and productID. CER records dependencies on accessed dates so results can be recalculated when dates change. Notes that accessing product delivery rule objects does not create precedents. Also introduces propagation behavior when CaseHeader or ProductDelivery data changes, including reassessment strategy handling.

children.title: Precedents Identified
children.summary: Accessing Product Delivery Rule Objects during CER calculations does not create precedents. The section explains that CER-based product delivery cases are converted on demand into a `ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase` rule object, which computes `determinationResult` using case data such as case IDs, dates, and productID. It also notes that the engine records dependencies from accessed input values, especially dates, enabling automatic recalculation when those dates change.

children.title: Propagation Processing
children.summary: Describes propagation behavior for CER-based product delivery cases. The propagator ignores cases configured to use Cúram Rules, and for CER products it can be controlled by the “Reassessment Strategy” setting. When CaseHeader or ProductDelivery data changes, the propagator loads the ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase rule object and updates it in memory. This section also notes that rule objects are converted on demand, determination calculations track dependencies on case dates, and accessing Product Delivery Rule Objects does not create precedents.

children.title: Example
children.summary: Example of CER-based product delivery case reassessment with strategy “Do not reassess closed cases.” It shows activation, dependency record creation, reassessment when expected end date changes, removal of dependency records on close, and rebuilding them on reactivation. It also illustrates how later evidence changes trigger reassessment and a new determination result. Useful for understanding the interaction among the Engine, Product Delivery Rule Object Converter/Propagator, and Dependency Manager.

Explains how CER-based product delivery cases are converted into `ProductEligibilityEntitlementRuleSet.ProductDeliveryCase` rule objects and propagated on data changes. The converter populates case fields from CaseHeader and ProductDelivery rows; `determinationResult` is calculated on demand and records date dependencies for reassessment. Accessing these rule objects does not create precedents. The propagator ignores Cúram Rules products and is governed by the product’s Reassessment Strategy, including “Do not reassess closed cases,” with an example showing close, reopen, and evidence-triggered reassessment behavior.

children.title: Rate Rule Objects
Overview
children.summary: Describes the Rate Rule Object Propagator for CER rate tables. It creates, maintains, and removes rate cell rule objects stored directly in CER database tables. Explains configuration requirements (propagator type “ROPT2006” and ratetable entries), cumulative behavior across configurations, and one detected config issue: missing rate table type code. Notes rates are not converted incrementally; they are propagated only during initial/full propagation or when rate changes are explicitly applied, and CER identifies precedents directly from stored rate values.

children.title: Configuration
children.summary: Explains the Rate Rule Object Propagator for CER rate tables. It creates, maintains, and removes rate cell rule objects stored directly in CER database tables. Configuration requires propagator type `ROPT2006` and `ratetable` entries matching rate table type codes; configurations are cumulative. Detects missing rate table type codes as configuration problems. Rates are not incrementally propagated; they are processed only during initial/full propagation or when explicitly applying rate changes to CER products.

children.title: Conversion Processing
children.summary: This section explains rate rule object handling in CER. It says the Rate Rule Object Propagator stores rate rule objects directly in CER database tables, uses propagator type “ROPT2006,” and applies to listed rate tables cumulatively. It notes configuration validation for missing rate table type codes. There is no separate converter for rate data; rate rule objects are read directly from the database during calculations. It also describes when rate propagation occurs: initial/full propagation or explicit application of rate changes.

children.title: Precedents Identified
children.summary: Describes the Rate Rule Object Propagator for CER rate tables. It creates, maintains, and removes rate cell rule objects stored directly in CER database tables, based on configurations of type “ROPT2006” listing ratetables by type code. Notes that rate data is not incrementally propagated; it is processed only during initial/full propagation or when rate changes are explicitly applied. Also states that precedent identification for accessed rate rule objects uses CER internal IDs directly.

children.title: Propagation Processing
children.summary: Describes the Rate Rule Object Propagator for CER rate tables. It creates, maintains, and removes rate cell rule objects stored directly in CER database tables, unlike other propagators. Configuration uses propagator type ROPT2006 with ratetable entries; configurations are cumulative. No rate-data converter exists, and rate objects are read directly during calculations. Propagation is non-incremental: rates are processed only during initial/full propagation or when the user explicitly applies rate changes, after which configured rate cells are read for all listed rate tables.

children.title: Example
children.summary: This section explains how rate table changes are propagated into RateCell rule objects and how the Dependency Manager records dependencies at the Attribute Value level. It notes that only top-level row/column rate cells and only their values (not min/max ranges) are propagated, with removed cells deleted and changes triggering reassessment tracking. The example shows an “Income Limits” rate table producing a valueTimeline with default zero before the effective date, then 10,000 and 12,000 over time, and illustrates dependency storage for a case entitlement.

Describes the Rate Rule Object Propagator for CER rate tables. It creates, updates, and removes RateCell rule objects stored directly in CER database tables. Configuration requires propagator type `ROPT2006` and matching `ratetable` type codes; multiple configurations are cumulative, and missing rate table type codes are flagged as errors. Rate data is not incrementally propagated: it is processed only during initial/full propagation or when applying rate changes. Precedents are identified directly from rate cell values, at the Attribute Value level.

children.title: Entity Rule Objects
Overview
children.summary: Explains Entity Rule Objects. The Entity Rule Object Converter maps generic database rows to CER rule objects for CRUD-style entity data, but not for rate tables or evidence. The Entity Rule Object Propagator listens for database writes and notifies the Dependency Manager of changes. Describes shared configuration: type ROPT2003, table and ruleset naming, optional canceled-row marking, and optional custom filters. Configurations are cumulative, and excluded tables cannot be propagated.

children.title: Configuration
children.summary: This section explains how to configure Entity Rule Objects for Cúram Express Rules. It covers the Entity Rule Object Converter and Propagator, including the required configuration structure: propagator type must be ROPT2003, entities are mapped by table name, propagation targets by ruleset, optional canceled-row markers, and optional row filters. It also notes that configurations are cumulative, and tables on the exclude list cannot be propagated.

children.title: Conversion Processing
children.summary: Explains how database rows are converted to rule objects in CER processing: matching database columns map to identically named rule attributes, unused columns are ignored, and the key column must map to a rule attribute used to identify the object. Conversion requires a modeled table with a single-column primary key of a supported type. The section also advises reusing existing rule sets/classes, keeping custom propagated rule classes minimal, using separate table elements for different filters, and lists configuration errors the converter detects.

children.title: Precedents Identified
children.summary: Explains which precedents CER identifies when Entity Rule Objects are accessed. A primary-key entity row search or any attribute access creates an Entity Row precedent tied to the entity, PK field, and sought value; it recalculates on row data changes, inserts, or deletes. A `readall` search creates a rule-class precedent for all objects of that class; it recalculates on inserts/deletes and when rows become or stop being logically deleted or matching a filter.

children.title: Propagation Processing
children.summary: Explains how the Entity Rule Object Propagator handles database row changes for entity rule objects, creating/modifying/removing in-memory rule objects and notifying the Dependency Manager. Describes which entity operation stereotypes are propagatable or ignored, including key exceptions for batch operations, ns SQL, and “no generated SQL” cases. Also covers the configurable response to non-propagatable operations via an environment variable (warn/ignore/error) and the use of an exclude list for tables that should never participate in propagation.

children.title: Example
children.summary: Example of Entity Rule Object Converter behavior with multiple rule sets mapped to the same Person table. Shows a custom MyRuleSet.MyPerson class with attributes concernRoleID, dateOfBirth, and dateOfBirthVerInd, alongside an existing ParticipantEntitiesRuleSet.Person mapping. Demonstrates that the converter populates only attributes present on the requested rule class from the same database row, with concernRoleID required as the primary key, while unmatched attributes like dateOfDeath or dateOfBirthVerInd are omitted depending on the class.

Explains Entity Rule Objects for CER CRUD-style entity data. The Entity Rule Object Converter maps database rows to rule objects; the Propagator tracks writes and notifies the Dependency Manager. Covers shared ROPT2003 configuration, table/ruleset mapping, optional logical-delete markers and filters, cumulative configs, and exclusion-list limits. Describes row-to-attribute conversion rules, required single-column primary keys, supported readall/match access and precedents, propagatable vs unsupported entity operations, error-level behavior, and an example showing the same Person row mapped to multiple rule classes.

children.title: Active Succession Set Rule Objects
Overview
children.summary: Explains the Active Succession Set Rule Object Converter and Propagator for Cúram evidence-to-CER rule processing. The converter turns activated succession sets of evidence records into a single CER rule object with timeline and non-timeline attributes; the propagator watches active evidence changes and notifies the Dependency Manager. Notes that pending evidence changes are ignored, targeted rule classes must extend PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveSuccessionSet, and parent/child relationships can be populated. Includes required configuration: propagator type ROPT2005, evidence types, and target rulesets.

children.title: Configuration
children.summary: This section explains the Active Succession Set Rule Object Converter and Propagator for CER rules. It describes how activated evidence succession sets are converted into a single timeline-aware rule object, with support for parent/child relationships and both dynamic and non-dynamic evidence. It also outlines the shared configuration requirements: propagator type must be ROPT2005, each evidence type must match the EvidenceType code table, and each target ruleset must be listed, optionally with a distinct rule class.

children.title: Conversion Processing
children.summary: Explains how the Active Succession Set Rule Object Converter populates CER rule objects from active evidence. Covers mapping evidence fields to rule attributes, handling timeline attributes via succession set lifetime and effective dates, and non-timeline attributes by using the latest version and logging inconsistencies. Describes automatic population of parent/child related succession set relationships, inherited attributes like successionID, caseID, exists, and evidenceDescriptorID, plus handling of in-edit changes and access restrictions for <readall>/<match> (caseID only).

children.title: Precedents Identified
children.summary: Explains how precedents are identified for Active Succession Set Rule Objects during CER calculations. Accessing these rule objects records precedents for Active Evidence (caseID of the owning case) and Rule Object Data Configurations (converter configuration use). Notes that in-edit evidence changes create precedent change items. Also describes propagation processing: the Propagator listens to evidence changes, updates rule objects in memory, and informs the Dependency Manager, which stores dependencies at the case level.

children.title: Propagation Processing
children.summary: Explains how Active Succession Set Rule Objects are handled when evidence changes: the propagator listens to Evidence Controller events, retrieves and updates rule objects in memory, and may create, modify, or remove them depending on the evidence action. It then notifies the Dependency Manager so impacts can be computed. Also warns not to use `<readall>` without `<match>`, and notes precedents for active evidence and rule object data configurations.

children.title: Example
children.summary: Worked example of Cúram Express Rules Active Succession Set behavior for temporal evidence. It models Income evidence with varying amount and employmentStatus, shows how evidence versions map to rule-object timelines on activation, changes, and end of employment, and illustrates reassessment after cancellations. The section then introduces child temporal evidence, Responsibility, with parent/child navigation between Income and Responsibility rule objects. Useful for understanding succession sets, timeline population, dependency-driven reassessment, and parent-child evidence conversion.

Defines Active Succession Set Rule Objects for CER: an ROPT2005 converter/propagator that turns activated evidence succession sets into one timeline-aware rule object and notifies the Dependency Manager on active evidence changes. Covers configuration rules, required extension of PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveSuccessionSet, and handling of dynamic/non-dynamic evidence, parent/child succession links, and precedents. Includes a detailed Income example showing activation, change, end, cancellation, and reassessment, then introduces child Responsibility evidence and navigation between parent Income and child Responsibility rule objects.

children.title: Active Evidence Row Rule Objects
Overview
children.summary: Describes the Active Evidence Row Rule Object Converter, which turns each active evidence row into its own CER rule object and can also link parent/child evidence versions. It applies to both dynamic and non-dynamic evidence, uses PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveEvidenceRow with correctionSetID as a unique identifier, and is mainly useful for non-temporal evidence. The section also explains required configuration: propagator type ROPT2004, evidence types listed by exact code table name, and target rulesets/rule classes; configurations are cumulative.

children.title: Configuration
children.summary: Defines configuration for the Active Evidence Row Rule Object Converter/propagator. It uses propagator type ROPT2004, requires each evidence type to appear under an evidence element matching the EvidenceType code table, and each target rule set to be listed in a nested ruleset element with optional rule-class override. Configurations are cumulative across multiple ROPT2004 entries, so any listed evidence type is processed; unlisted types are ignored. Also notes this converter is suited to non-temporal evidence and can populate parent/child rule relationships.

children.title: Conversion Processing
Restrictions on Access
children.summary: This section explains access limits when using CER <readall>/<match> on Active Evidence Row Rule Objects. Only caseID may be used as the retrievedattribute; using any other attribute causes a runtime exception. It recommends using <filter> to narrow results after matching on caseID, and notes that only rule classes directly mapped by data configuration can be read. It warns not to use <readall> without <match> because it can retrieve too many objects and store no dependency.

Explains how the Active Evidence Row Rule Object Converter populates CER rule objects from active evidence rows. It maps evidence fields to same-named rule attributes, ignores unmatched fields, and can populate related parent/child evidence as rule objects or lists, with different behavior for 0/1/many related versions. It also lists inherited ActiveEvidenceRow attributes such as correctionSetID, caseID, description, effectiveDate, evidenceDescriptorID, and successionID, and notes configuration problems detected during conversion.

children.title: Precedents Identified
children.summary: This section explains how Active Evidence Row Rule Objects are accessed in CER calculations and what precedents are identified. It covers handling of in-edit evidence changes, including special support during manual determinations. It also states access restrictions: retrievedattribute must be caseID, only directly mapped rule classes may be used, and <readall> must always include <match>. It recommends using <filter> for narrower selection. Useful if you need ruleset behavior, runtime constraints, or dependency/precedent identification for active evidence row data.

children.title: Propagation Processing
children.summary: Explains how Active Evidence Row Rule Objects are propagated when evidence changes are applied. The propagator listens to Evidence Controller events, retrieves and updates rule objects in memory, and may create, modify, or remove them depending on evidence activation, corrections, changes of circumstance, or cancellation. It notifies the Dependency Manager of changed active evidence so impacts can be determined, with dependencies stored at the case level via the owning caseID. Includes a table of precedents and an example showing independent changes to income amount and employment status.

children.title: Example
children.summary: This section explains Active Evidence Row Rule Objects, including how precedents are identified, how evidence changes trigger recalculation, and how the Dependency Manager stores case-level dependencies. It gives a detailed Income evidence example showing initial activation, subsequent changes of circumstances, end-dating, cancellation, and resulting rule object values across correction sets. It then introduces a new Responsibility child evidence type under Income, indicating navigation to parent Income rule objects for related rules.

Explains the Active Evidence Row Rule Object Converter/Propagator (ROPT2004), which turns each active evidence row into its own CER rule object, supports both dynamic and static evidence, and can populate parent/child evidence relationships. Covers configuration requirements and cumulative mappings, conversion behavior, inherited attributes, in-edit evidence handling, access restrictions via <readall>/<match>, and precedents/dependency tracking. Includes a detailed Income example showing activation, changes, ending, cancellation, and introduction of child Responsibility evidence.

Covers CER eligibility/entitlement rule object types and how converters/propagators feed the Dependency Manager. Includes product delivery cases (on-demand conversion, no precedents, reassessment strategy and closed-case behavior), rate tables (ROPT2006, cumulative config, direct DB storage, non-incremental propagation, attribute-value precedents), entity rule objects (ROPT2003 CRUD mappings), and active evidence row rule objects (ROPT2004, one rule object per active evidence row, parent/child evidence, access restrictions, precedents, and detailed Income examples through activation, changes, ending, cancellation, and Responsibility child evidence).

children.title: Data Configuration Problems
children.summary: This section introduces data configuration problems for configurable converters and propagators. It explains that their behavior when configuration issues are detected is controlled by the `curam.ruleobjectpropagation.configuration.errorlevel` environment variable. The surrounding example shows how evidence design and rule class configuration create parent/child rule object links between `Income` and `Responsibility`, but the section itself focuses on configuration error handling.

children.title: Data Access Points
Normal Conversion
children.summary: Explains normal conversion in CER: when CER performs a rule-object search, it invokes the appropriate rule object converter based on the rule class. The converter reads active evidence from the application database to populate in-memory CER rule objects for further calculations. It specifically uses active succession set rule objects and active evidence row rule objects. This retrieval supports assessment, snapshot, and manual check determinations when “active evidence only” is selected.

children.title: Temporary Access to In-Edit Evidence Changes
children.summary: Explains a special converter mode for manual determinations based on in-edit evidence changes. CER rule object converters for active succession sets and active evidence rows can temporarily use pending additions, modifications, and exclusions instead of only active evidence when a case worker requests a manual check. The engine starts a new CER session and retrieves the determination result using this in-edit-aware calculation. The section also notes incremental propagation, which tracks internal data changes for recalculation, and warns that non-propagatable writes or external database updates require manual reassessment.

children.title: Incremental Propagation
children.summary: Explains incremental propagation, the mechanism that listens for internal changes to evidence or mapped entity rows during normal application use and notifies the Dependency Manager so it can find dependents for recalculation. Also notes limits: some precedent data changes are not detected automatically, including non-propagatable writes and database updates made outside standard modeled entity operations (e.g. SQL scripts or other systems). In such cases, manual reassessment of potentially affected cases is required.

children.title: Bulk Maintenance of Rate Rule Objects
children.summary: Explains how CER stores rate table rule objects as database “mirror” data and why they can become out of sync. Covers four causes: unapplied admin changes, first production deployment with no objects yet, development databases without initial objects, and external data changes. Describes remedies: Apply Changes for small updates, RateCreateInitialRuleObjects or build prepare.application.data for initial creation, and FullPropagationToRuleObjects for larger outside-API changes. Notes initial propagation is controlled by RuleObjectPropagatorControl and a required DMX control row.

Overviews where CER rule object converters/propagators get data from the application database. Normal conversion uses active evidence only for assessments, snapshots, and manual checks. A special mode lets manual checks use in-edit pending evidence additions, changes, and removals in a new CER session. Incremental propagation tracks internal evidence/entity-row changes for recalculation, but not external SQL or non-propagatable writes. Also covers rate rule object mirror data maintenance, including causes of staleness and remedies like Apply Changes, initial propagation, and FullPropagationToRuleObjects.

children.title: Logging
children.summary: This section explains logging for Cúram rule object propagators and converters. It says configuration problems are automatically written to application logs and recommends monitoring warnings. Detailed tracing can be enabled via the curam.trace.ruleobjectpropagation and curam.trace environment variables, with levels trace_off, trace_on, trace_verbose, and trace_ultra_verbose; the more verbose setting wins. It also lists what may be logged, such as database writes, matching searches, rule object create/modify/remove actions, and attribute values set by converters.

children.title: Supported Domain Types
children.summary: Explains how business data is converted into CER rule objects for supported domain types. It says most fundamental application domain types can be used, and a table maps each domain to the correct CER data type, including any conversion logic and default values for non-sourced data (for example, timeline before-start/after-end values). This section is mainly a reference for choosing the right CER rule attribute type and understanding conversion behavior.

children.title: Propagator Filter Hook
children.summary: Explains the CER Propagator Filter Hook for suppressing propagation events so they don’t reach CER or generate PCIs, useful to stop certain data changes from triggering reassessments. Describes implementing `PropagatorFilterHook.shouldIgnore(...)`, which receives the propagator, listener, session, and `DatabaseWriteDetails`, and may return true to ignore a change. Notes multiple hook implementations can be bound per propagator type, any true result suppresses propagation, and shows Guice `MapBinder`-based binding via delegate subclasses.

Explains how Cúram uses CER rule object converters to pull business data into rule objects and propagators to notify the Dependency Manager when source data changes. Introduces matching converter/propagator pairs, shared configuration, and an initial assessment walkthrough covering product setup, rate tables, person/case/evidence conversion, and activation. Also covers runtime framework, supported rule object/domain mappings, configuration error handling, data access, logging, bulk rate-object maintenance, and the propagator filter hook for suppressing selected propagation events.

children.title: 1.8 How Determinations Are Stored
Introduction
children.summary: Explains how the Engine stores determination results after calculation. It typically writes a row to CREOLECaseDetermination, plus related child tables, and may store a snapshot of CER rule objects used. Eligibility/entitlement data is stored in CaseDecision and linked via CREOLECaseDecision so Cúram Financials can generate financial components later. Notes that these tables are usually not accessed directly. Also outlines the chapter’s structure: database tables, decision periods, and determination comparison strategies.

children.title: The Database Tables
CREOLECaseDetermination
children.summary: Describes the CREOLECaseDetermination database table, the main store for each determination result. Covers common fields populated for all determinations: ID, case ID, date/time, type, created by user, and the XML result data ID. Also details type-specific population for Manual Check, Snapshot, and Case Assessment records, including assessment reason/status, snapshot reason, and evidenceUsed, with notes on which code tables allow extensions.

children.title: CREOLECaseDeterminationData
children.summary: Describes the CREOLECaseDeterminationData table, which stores XML for determinations. It typically has two rows per CREOLECaseDetermination: one for the determination result XML (eligibility/entitlement, decision factors/details, errors) and one for a CER rule-object snapshot used for audit and later inspection. Snapshots are always stored for assessment and snapshot determinations, and for manual check determinations only if enabled by configuration. Very large XML can overflow into chained overflow rows.

children.title: CaseDecision
children.summary: Explains how the Engine stores determination results in the CaseDecision table for assessment/eligibility cases. It records varying eligibility and entitlement over the life of a case, superseding prior stored determinations for new assessments, and creates rows only when eligibility/entitlement changes, not merely decision reasons. Notes that key decision factors are deprecated. Also states CaseDecision remains used for legacy Cúram Rules cases, with some reserved attributes, and shows core field population such as caseDecisionID, caseID, and decisionDate.

children.title: CaseDecisionObjective
children.summary: Defines the CaseDecisionObjective entity, which stores only attained (entitled) objectives for a parent CaseDecision; non-entitled objectives are not stored. The Engine creates rows for each entitlement change over a case’s lifetime, with each row representing one objective attained for the decision period. The section begins the mapping of stored attributes for CER-based cases, centered on determination data from an AbstractObjectiveTimeline rule object.

children.title: CaseDecisionObjectiveTag
children.summary: Explains how the engine populates CaseDecisionObjectiveTag rows for CER-based cases. These rows represent the frequency at which an attained objective can be delivered and are created for each CaseDecisionObjective. It details the stored attributes: primary key, parent case decision ID, objectiveTagID from AbstractTagTimeline.tagType/AbstractTagType.tagTypeID, value from AbstractTagTimeline.valueTimeline with numeric-to-string conversion if needed, and description left blank. Also notes these tags are used to calculate financial schedule amounts.

children.title: CREOLECaseDecision
children.summary: Defines CREOLECaseDecision as the link between a CREOLECaseDetermination and a CaseDecision, recording each period of constant eligibility and entitlement in a determination. Explains that the Engine creates a CaseDecision row for every decision period and stores child records for changes over time. A “change” includes changes to eligibility results, attained objectives, or objective target, related reference, or tag values. Also notes that products can configure determination comparison strategy to control when a new result is different enough to replace the previous one.

Describes the engine’s database tables for storing determination data: CREOLECaseDetermination (core record per determination, with type-specific fields for manual check, snapshot, and case assessment), CREOLECaseDeterminationData (XML result and rule-object snapshot storage, including overflow handling), CaseDecision (time-varying eligibility/entitlement periods for assessment cases, with superseding rules), CaseDecisionObjective (attained objectives), CaseDecisionObjectiveTag (objective delivery frequency tags), and CREOLECaseDecision (linking determinations to decision periods). Includes field mappings, current/superseded status behavior, and configurable snapshot storage for manual checks.

children.title: Decision Periods
children.summary: This section explains how Cúram stores “decision periods” in CaseDecision and CREOLECaseDecision when eligibility or entitlement changes over a determination’s lifetime. A new row is created whenever eligibility changes, attained objectives change, or an attained objective’s target, related reference, or tag values change. It also notes that CER-based products can configure comparison strategies to decide when a new determination result is sufficiently different to replace the previous stored result.

children.title: Determination Comparison Strategies
Strategy Implementations Included with the Engine
children.summary: The Engine provides built-in determination comparison strategies used to decide whether a new eligibility/entitlement snapshot is stored as new XML data or linked to existing data. Products must select a strategy via CREOLEProduct.dmx or the admin UI, using the DeterminationComparisonStrategy interface. The section notes that different explanations can trigger a new stored determination even when the underlying result is unchanged. It introduces the included strategy implementations and highlights “Compare all user-facing data” as a useful default during early development.

children.title: Developing your own Strategy Implementation
children.summary: Explains how to create a custom strategy implementation for comparing eligibility/entitlement determinations when built-in Engine options are insufficient. It outlines the required steps: add a new DeterminationCompStrategy code table entry, implement DeterminationComparisonStrategy, bind the code to the implementation in a custom Guice module, build the application, and configure the product to use the new strategy. Also introduces the next chapter on scheduling financials, describing the financial scheduler’s role between the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine and the Financial Manager.

Explains how the Engine decides whether a newly calculated eligibility/entitlement determination should overwrite or reuse stored XML data. Products must configure a DeterminationComparisonStrategy, either in CREOLEProduct.dmx or via the admin UI. The strategy can treat determinations as different even when eligibility is unchanged if explanations differ. Notes the built-in “Compare all user-facing data” strategy as a useful early-development default and introduces included strategy implementations.

Explains how the Engine stores determination results and when it writes new rows. Core storage is CREOLECaseDetermination, with XML in CREOLECaseDeterminationData and links to time-varying eligibility/entitlement data in CaseDecision via CREOLECaseDecision. CaseDecisionObjective and CaseDecisionObjectiveTag store attained objectives and delivery-frequency tags. Decision periods are created when eligibility, objectives, target/reference, or tag values change. Also covers configurable determination comparison strategies that decide whether a new result is distinct enough to overwrite or reuse stored XML.

children.title: 1.9 Scheduling Financials
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces the financial scheduler in Cúram 8.1.2, which converts eligibility/entitlement results and case deductions into financial components for the Financial Manager to process into payments or bills. It outlines that the chapter covers scheduling for eligible case decisions, case deduction items, and payment corrections, and that each schedule will be described by how it looks, works, and is used. The excerpt also includes setup guidance for defining custom determination comparison strategies via code tables, a comparison strategy class, and Guice binding.

children.title: Scheduling Financials for Eligible Case Decisions
How It Looks
children.summary: Explains how financial schedules are displayed and constructed for eligible case decisions. A financial instruction shows total payment, line items, nominee, and delivery details. Example: a $407.15 benefit payment from 22 June to 10 July is split into a ramp-up partial-week payment and recurring weekly payments. The section also introduces factors affecting scheduling, including case decision objectives, nominee component assignments, delivery patterns, decision period, and objective tags, and notes that eligible decisions are first turned into virtual components matching the decision dates.

children.title: How It Works
Considering Case Decision Objectives
children.summary: This section explains how eligible case decisions are converted into virtual components for financial scheduling. Each case decision objective becomes one virtual component, with start and end dates matching the decision; open-ended decisions produce open-ended virtual components. These virtual components are the basis for later financial schedules, instruction line items, and payment processing.

children.title: Considering Nominee Component Assignments
children.summary: Explains how an initial virtual component is refined after creation: first split by nominee component assignment changes, then by nominee delivery pattern changes. Example cases show how one eligibility period can become multiple nominee- and pattern-specific virtual components. It then begins describing how each virtual component is further split into financial components based on delivery frequency, introducing ramp-up and recurring financial components. Use this node for the mechanics of turning component cover periods into nominee- and schedule-specific financial periods.

children.title: Considering Nominee Delivery Patterns
children.summary: This section explains how nominee-specific virtual components are further split by changes in delivery pattern during the cover period. Example: James changes from weekly EFT in advance to daily EFT, while Linda uses weekly check in advance, producing separate virtual components for each pattern period. It then shows how these are realized into financial schedules. It also introduces financial component cover periods, including ramp-up components when a virtual component starts mid-cycle and recurring components for full delivery cycles.

children.title: Calculating Financial Component Cover Periods
children.summary: Explains how each virtual component is split into financial components based on delivery frequency. Up to three financial components may result per virtual component: a ramp-up component from the virtual start date until the first complete delivery period, a recurring component covering all full cycles within the cover period, and potentially another final component for any remaining partial period. Useful for understanding how component cover periods are converted into realizable financial schedules.

children.title: Calculating Open Ended Financial Component Cover Periods
children.summary: Explains how open-ended cases affect financial component cover periods. If the last decision is eligible and open ended, the system creates a recurring open-ended financial component instead of a ramp-down component. Using a weekly Monday delivery pattern example, the cover is split into a ramp-up period and then a complete delivery-cycle period “until further notice.” When an end date is later added, the open-ended component is replaced by a bounded one. Also notes that component amounts are based on the relevant cover period, or the first complete cycle for recurring/open-ended components.

children.title: Calculating Financial Component Amounts
children.summary: This section explains how to determine financial component amounts and effective dates after components are identified. For ramp-up and ramp-down components, the amount is based on the component’s own cover period; for recurring or open-ended components, it is based on the first complete delivery cycle. It uses the Linda example to show three split periods across a weekly Monday payment pattern, including how an open-ended case produces recurring components until an end date is later added.

children.title: Calculating Financial Component Effective Dates
children.summary: Explains how financial component amounts are calculated from case decision objective tags by frequency (daily, weekly, bi-monthly, monthly, yearly), including ramp-up/ramp-down handling where larger tags are applied first. Gives examples showing mixed daily/weekly calculations and a ramp-up example with monthly, weekly, and daily tags. Also describes how financial component effective dates are set from cover period type and delivery pattern: Issue in Advance uses the first covered delivery period start; Issue in Arrears uses the following period start; Issue for Full Month begins the full-month delivery period.

Explains how eligible case decisions become financial schedules: first into virtual components, then split by nominee assignment changes, delivery pattern changes, and finally by delivery frequency into financial components (ramp-up, recurring, ramp-down/open-ended). Covers open-ended cases, component amounts based on cover periods or first full cycle, and effective dates determined by issue-in-advance/arreas/full-month rules. Includes examples showing how weekly/daily amounts are calculated from case decision objective tags and how partial periods are handled.

children.title: How to Use It
Mandatory Information
children.summary: This section explains the mandatory inputs needed to generate financial schedules for an eligible case decision. It covers Case Decision Objectives, defined in the product’s CER rule set as awardable outcomes, and Case Decision Objective Tags, which specify how objectives are awarded over time periods and may be amounts or formulas. It also notes that eligibility rule sets can include multiple objectives and multiple tags per objective.

children.title: Optional Information
children.summary: This section covers optional financial setup for eligible cases: required financial code tables for correct schedule/transaction generation, nominee component assignments, nominee delivery patterns, and the “Allow Open Ended Cases” product indicator. It explains that assigning components or delivery patterns to multiple nominees over time creates separate financial components, and that delivery patterns control payment frequency, cover period type, and offsets. It also notes the default open-ended setting and how disabling it forces end dates on cases and financial schedules.

This section explains what must be configured to generate financial schedules for an eligible case decision. Mandatory inputs are the case decision objectives and objective tags defined in the product’s CER rule set, which determine what is awarded and how it is awarded over time. Optional setup includes financial code table customization, nominee component assignments, nominee delivery patterns, and the “Allow Open Ended Cases” indicator, which defaults to Yes and controls whether cases and resulting financial schedules may be open ended.

Explains how eligible case decisions are converted into financial schedules and instruction line items. The process creates virtual components from decision objectives, then splits them by nominee assignment, delivery pattern, and delivery frequency into ramp-up, recurring, and ramp-down/open-ended financial components. It covers how amounts are calculated from objective tags and how effective dates vary by issue mode (advance, arrears, full month, offsets). Also lists required configuration: objectives/tags and financial code tables, plus optional nominee assignments, delivery patterns, and open-ended case support.

children.title: Scheduling Financials for Case Deductions
How It Looks
children.summary: Explains how financial information appears to a case worker when deductions are taken from payments. Describes a sample financial instruction showing payment and deduction line items, nominee details, and delivery details. Illustrates how the financial scheduler uses eligibility/entitlement results and case deduction items to create financial components, including a one-time ramp-up component for a partial period and recurring weekly payment components.

children.title: How It Works
Considering Case Deduction Items
children.summary: This section explains how active case deduction items are converted into financial schedule components. It says only deductions ending after the case’s last paid-to date are considered, then distinguishes fixed deductions, which affect one nominee, from variable deductions, which may affect multiple nominees based on component assignments and delivery patterns. Using an example case, it shows a fixed $12 deduction for Lisa and a 20% Income Assistance deduction that must be split between Lisa and Paul because the component assignment changes during the deduction period.

children.title: Considering Deduction Types
children.summary: Explains how Cúram turns case deduction items into financial schedule components, focusing on deduction type. Fixed deductions affect one nominee and become a single deduction financial component; variable deductions can affect multiple nominees depending on targeted components and changing nominee component assignments, so they may be split into nominee-specific parts across the cover period. Example shows a fixed $12 weekly deduction for Lisa and a variable 20% Income Assistance deduction split between Lisa and Paul due to a component assignment change.

children.title: Calculating Deduction Cover Periods
children.summary: Explains how deduction financial component cover periods are calculated: they start on the case deduction item start date, or the day after the last paid date if the start date is earlier, and end on the item end date or the case expected end date if no end date is set. Also notes required setup for generating financial schedules: the deduction item must be activated and valid, and optional nominee component assignments can split a variable deduction into separate nominee-specific financial components.

This section explains how case deduction items become deduction financial components for financial schedules. It considers whether deductions are fixed or variable, nominee component assignments, nominee delivery patterns, the deduction period, and the case’s last paid-to date. Active deductions ending after the last paid date are processed; ended deductions are ignored. Fixed deductions map to one nominee, while variable deductions can be split across nominees if component assignments change during the cover period. Example: a $12 fixed deduction for Lisa and a 20% Income Assistance deduction split between Lisa and Paul.

children.title: How to Use It
Mandatory Information
children.summary: This section explains how financial schedules are generated for case deduction items. It covers deduction cover periods, including how the start date is adjusted if it falls before the last paid date and how the end date is determined from the deduction item or case expected end date. It states the mandatory requirement that the case deduction item must be added and activated, with any end date after the last paid date. It also notes optional nominee component assignments, which can split deductions across nominees, and mentions payment correction schedules.

children.title: Optional Information
children.summary: Explains optional inputs needed to generate financial schedules for a case deduction item. Focuses on nominee component assignments: each case component must be assigned to a nominee, and components can be reassigned over specific dates or periods. If a variable deduction item targets a component assigned to multiple nominees during its life, separate deduction financial components are created for each nominee. Also notes that Engine reassessment information is used to create payment correction financial schedules.

Explains what information is needed to generate financial schedules for a case deduction item. Mandatory: the deduction item must be added and activated, and any end date must be after the last paid date. Optional: nominee component assignments can split a component across nominees over time, creating separate deduction financial components. Also notes that Engine reassessment information is used to generate payment correction financial schedules, and clarifies how deduction cover periods are calculated from the start date, last paid date, and end date or expected case end date.

Explains how active case deduction items are converted into deduction financial schedules. Covers fixed vs. variable deductions, nominee and component assignments, delivery patterns, deduction cover periods, and last paid-to date filtering. Fixed deductions map to one nominee; variable deductions can split across nominees if assignments change during the cover period. Includes an example where Lisa has a $12 fixed deduction and a 20% Income Assistance deduction split between Lisa and Paul, alongside how these appear in financial instructions with payment line items.

children.title: Scheduling Financials for Payment Corrections
How It Looks
children.summary: Explains how payment corrections appear to case workers when a reassessment finds a past payment was incorrect. Describes overpayment, underpayment, and net-zero corrections, and how reassessment data is stored and broken down by nominee and component to generate evidence, financial components, and instruction line items. Uses an example where Income Assistance and Medical Assistance payments are recalculated, producing a $17.12 overpayment displayed in a Payment Correction case, with component-level amounts of $6.41 and $10.71.

children.title: How It Works
Considering Payment Correction Types
children.summary: This section explains how payment correction types drive financial schedule creation. It defines overpayment, underpayment, and net-zero corrections, including how mixed component results are totaled. It also describes three correction products: Payment Correction (supports all types and can create component-level schedules), Overpayment (single liability schedule for overpayments only), and Underpayment (single benefit schedule for underpayments only). By default, the Payment Correction product is used, but an application property can switch to the other products.

children.title: Considering Correction Products
children.summary: Explains how payment correction financial schedules are created and how product choice affects them. Covers the default two-step process, an alternative underpayment approach on the original benefit case, and factors influencing representation of corrections. Defines overpayment, underpayment, and net-zero correction types. Describes three correction products: Payment Correction (supports all types and creates component-level schedules), Overpayment (single liability schedule for total overpaid amount), and Underpayment (single benefit schedule for total underpaid amount). Notes an application property can switch from the default Payment Correction product.

children.title: Considering Nominees
children.summary: Explains how nominees are assigned on Payment Correction cases for overpayment, underpayment, and net zero corrections. Overpayments use the original benefit case’s primary client as nominee with delivery pattern “Once-off by Invoice.” Underpayments usually use the original underpaid nominee, may add a second nominee if different from the primary client, and are paid once only if the component assignment covers the full cover period. Also notes a configuration setting, “Use Rolled Up Reassessment Products,” which switches between the Payment Correction product and legacy over/underpayment products.

Explains how payment correction financial schedules are generated. By default, this is a two-step process: create a product delivery case from reassessment evidence, then activate it to produce schedules. Underpayments can alternatively be delivered directly on the original benefit case. The section defines overpayment, underpayment, and net-zero correction types and notes that mixed component amounts are totaled. It also compares correction products: Payment Correction (all types, per-component schedules), Overpayment (single liability), and Underpayment (single benefit), with a property allowing product selection.

children.title: How to Use It
When will an Overpayment Correction case be created?
children.summary: Explains when overpayment correction cases are created and how configuration controls the outcome. If “Use Rolled Up Reassessment Products” is set, the system may create either a payment correction case or an overpayment case. “Automatic Overpayment Case Processing” then determines whether the case is auto-created, auto-approved/activated with liabilities generated, or not created at all (instead a task alerts the user). Also notes related underpayment correction behavior and two application properties that influence case creation.

children.title: When will an Underpayment Correction case be created?
children.summary: Explains when an underpayment correction is delivered as a separate case versus on the original benefit case. Behavior depends on the Use Rolled Up Reassessment Products setting and Automatic Underpayment Case Creation. A separate underpayment/payment correction case can be auto-created and manually or automatically processed, or the system can create an underpayment financial component on the original case. Also covers application properties that affect case creation when outstanding liabilities exist or nominee differences are checked.

children.title: When will a Net Zero Correction case be created?
children.summary: Net Zero Correction cases are created when overpaid and underpaid components in a reassessment exactly offset, so the correction nets to zero. This is only possible when using the Payment Correction product and the “Use Rolled Up Reassessment Products” setting is NO. These cases are automatically created, approved, activated, and financially generated without user intervention. The section also notes that payment corrections can arise from changes in circumstances affecting already paid periods, leading to under- or overpayments.

Explains the configuration that controls how payment correction financial schedules are generated. The main switch is Use Rolled Up Reassessment Products: NO uses Payment Correction products, YES uses legacy overpayment/underpayment products. It then details when overpayment and underpayment corrections become separate cases, when processing is automatic vs manual, and when underpayments stay on the original benefit case. Also covers application properties for outstanding liabilities and nominee-difference checks, which can force separate underpayment cases.

Explains how reassessment data drives payment correction financial schedules. A correction may be overpayment, underpayment, or net-zero, with nominee/component breakdown stored to create evidence, financial components, and instruction line items. Describes the two-step default flow: create a Payment Correction case, then activate it; underpayments can also be delivered on the original benefit case. Covers product choices (Payment Correction vs legacy Overpayment/Underpayment), nominee handling, and configuration such as Use Rolled Up Reassessment Products and automatic case creation rules.

Explains Cúram’s financial scheduler, which converts eligibility/entitlement results and case deductions into financial components and instruction line items for the Financial Manager. Covers how eligible case decisions are split by nominee assignment, delivery pattern, and delivery frequency into ramp-up, recurring, ramp-down, or open-ended components. Also summarizes case deduction scheduling and payment correction processing, including overpayment, underpayment, and net-zero cases, product choices, nominee handling, and key configuration such as Use Rolled Up Reassessment Products and automatic case creation settings.

children.title: 1.10 Reassessment - Handling Changes in Circumstance
Case-level reassessment
Reassessment Aggregation
The Workflow
Source Location
children.summary: Explains Cúram 8.1.2 reassessment aggregation, which by default groups multiple dependent-triggered case reassessments into one workflow-based reassessment to reduce conflicts. Can be disabled via `curam.case.reassessment.aggregation=NO`, reverting to Deferred Processes. Describes diagnostic tracing in `DependencyTrace` and status indications on Current Determination. Details the Reassessment Aggregation workflow, using `ReassessmentQueue` and `ReassessmentQueueControl`, with steps: claim queue, reassess case, release queue, then loop if more pending changes remain.

children.title: Enactment
children.summary: Explains the Reassessment Aggregation workflow’s enactment when the Dependency Manager processes precedent changes. If enabled, case assessment dependents delegate reassessment to new workflow instances, which queue cases and change sets in ReassessmentQueue. The workflow then claims the queue via ReassessmentQueueControl, performs CER reassessment, marks processed change sets complete, releases the claim, and loops if more pending entries exist. Notes the feature is enabled by default and can be disabled with curam.case.reassessment.aggregation=NO.

children.title: Default Behavior
children.summary: Describes the default Reassessment Aggregation behavior in Cúram 8.1.2. The feature is enabled by default; it can be disabled via the curam.case.reassessment.aggregation property set to “NO”, which reverts reassessments to Deferred Processes. Explains how the workflow prevents concurrent reassessment of the same case using ReassessmentQueue and ReassessmentQueueControl, with steps to claim the queue, reassess the case, release the queue, and loop if more pending change sets remain. Also notes DependencyTrace diagnostics for reassessment status.

Describes the Cúram 8.1.2 Reassessment Aggregation workflow that ensures only one process reassesses a case at a time. It is enabled by default and can be disabled with `curam.case.reassessment.aggregation=NO`, reverting to Deferred Processes. The workflow coordinates via `ReassessmentQueue` and `ReassessmentQueueControl`: claim the queue, reassess the case, mark change sets complete, release the queue, then loop if more pending changes remain. Also notes `DependencyTrace` diagnostics and Current Determination status messages.

children.title: The Database Tables
ReassessmentQueue
children.summary: Describes the ReassessmentQueue table used by the Reassessment Aggregation workflow to track reassessment requests per case. Each record stores reassessmentID, caseID, timestamps, status (Pending, Complete, Deferred To Batch), triggering precedentChangeSetID, and queueControlID. Explains that multiple requests for the same case can be aggregated into one reassessment, and errors mark records as Deferred To Batch for batch processing. Also references ReassessmentQueueControl, which tracks reassessment ownership and allows only one Pending reassessment per case at a time.

children.title: ReassessmentQueueControl
children.summary: Describes the ReassessmentQueueControl table used by the Reassessment Aggregation workflow to manage case reassessments. It explains that each row records a case reassessment that started, and possibly finished; a case can have many rows, but only one may be Pending at a time, ensuring only one reassessment process runs for that case concurrently. Also notes related workflow behavior: errors mark ReassessmentQueue and ReassessmentQueueControl as “Deferred To Batch” for later Dependency Manager batch processing.

Defines the database tables used by the Reassessment Aggregation workflow to coordinate case reassessments: ReassessmentQueue and ReassessmentQueueControl. ReassessmentQueue stores each reassessment request with IDs, timestamps, status (Pending, Complete, Deferred To Batch), triggering precedentChangeSetID, and queueControlID, and can aggregate multiple requests for the same case into one reassessment. ReassessmentQueueControl tracks reassessment ownership per case, allowing many entries but only one Pending at a time. Errors mark both tables Deferred To Batch for later Dependency Manager batch processing.

Explains Cúram 8.1.2 reassessment aggregation, a workflow that prevents multiple concurrent reassessments of the same case by coordinating Dependency Manager requests through ReassessmentQueue and ReassessmentQueueControl. It is enabled by default and can be disabled with curam.case.reassessment.aggregation=NO, reverting to deferred processes. Covers the workflow steps (claim queue, reassess case, release queue, loop for pending changes), error handling to Deferred To Batch, DependencyTrace diagnostics, and the table schemas/statuses used to track queued, completed, and batched reassessments.

Explains case-level reassessment in Cúram: which data changes trigger reassessment, how the Dependency Manager identifies affected dependents, and when reassessment runs in workflow versus deferred processing. Describes Reassessment Aggregation, a workflow that prevents concurrent reassessment of the same case by coordinating ReassessmentQueue and ReassessmentQueueControl. Covers default enabled behavior, disabling via curam.case.reassessment.aggregation=NO, retry/loop logic, error fallback to Deferred To Batch, diagnostics in DependencyTrace, and key table statuses for queued, complete, and deferred requests.

children.title: Bulk Reassessment
Types of Change that Cause Bulk Reassessment
Product Configuration
children.summary: This section explains that product configuration changes can affect case assessment results, but administrators can make them in a sandbox and publish them later. It covers product period changes such as adding/removing periods, changing dates, and changing the linked eligibility/entitlement, key decision factor, or decision details rule classes. It notes that unpublished changes do not affect processing, published changes trigger an informational message and may require bulk reassessment. It also mentions a deprecated key decision factors feature and that reassessment strategy changes need extra processing.

children.title: CER Rules used by your Product
children.summary: This section explains that changes to CER rule sets used by a product can affect assessment determinations for product cases. It sits within the broader lifecycle of admin-managed changes: edits can be made in a sandbox with no system effect until published, after which they take effect and may require bulk reassessment. It also notes that product configuration changes, especially product period changes, can impact cases, and that key decision factors are deprecated.

children.title: Rule Object Data Configurations
children.summary: This section explains that any change to configurable rule object data configurations can affect product delivery cases because CER may use different entities/evidence during rule execution. The Dependency Manager treats all such changes as potentially case-impacting, even when unrelated to assessments, and will report no affected cases if appropriate. It also notes the administration sandbox/publish workflow: unpublished data configuration changes do not affect processing until published. The section begins the next topic, rate tables, which can also affect cases.

children.title: Rate Tables
children.summary: This section explains that changes to rate tables configured to populate RateCell rule objects can affect product delivery cases. It highlights that impacts typically occur when an existing rate value changes or a new effective period starts. It also points readers to “Implementing Rate Tables” in the configuration guide for details.

Lists system-wide changes that can trigger bulk reassessment rather than case-level reassessment: product configuration, CER rules used by the product, rule object data configurations, and rate tables. Explains that admins work in a sandbox and changes have no effect until published; publication logs an informational message and may require batch reassessment. Product changes include product period edits and rule-class re-pointing; CER and data config changes are broadly treated as potentially case-impacting, though Dependency Manager may find no affected cases.

children.title: Approaches to Identifying and Reassessing All Affected Cases
Requirements for Bulk Reassessment
children.summary: This section lists the main requirements for bulk reassessment after system-wide changes: complete consistency (all affected cases must be found and reassessed), efficiency (only necessary reassessments), concurrency (parallel batch processing and online use at the same time), and business-specific control (custom ordering, selective reassessment, and extra per-case processing such as correspondence). It also notes that rate table changes are handled specially via the Engine’s “Apply Changes” option, with no system-wide publication step.

children.title: Multiple Reassessments during a Case's Lifetime
children.summary: Explains how reassessment works across a case’s lifetime and why bulk reassessment may be repeated without changing outcomes. Each reassessment recalculates the determination, compares it to the stored result, and only updates financial schedules or triggers over/under-payment corrections if different. Notes that “needless” reassessments are less costly than changed ones but still add overhead. Lists common reassessment triggers: lifecycle events, manual reassessment, case-level evidence/personal detail changes, financial batch forcing reassessment, and bulk reassessment.

children.title: Driving the Identification of Affected Cases
children.summary: Explains how to identify and reassess cases affected by published system-wide data changes. Covers timing concerns, concurrent online use, and possible transaction failures during bulk reassessment. Describes bottom-up identification via Dependency Manager batch suite, top-down identification by case facets, or a combination. Compares three approaches: Dependency Manager only, top-down plus Dependency Manager cleanup, and top-down only. Highlights benefits, limitations, need for custom batch processing, handling of additional business processing, and risks of missed or needless reassessments.

children.title: Reassessment Processing
children.summary: This section explains bulk reassessment processing after cases have been identified, including streamed batch reassessment of each case. It warns that extra business logic at reassessment time (via engine hook points or custom top-down code) may create precedent change items and require a Dependency Manager batch run. It also covers when to implement a custom bulk reassessment batch process versus using the built-in one. The included CREOLEBulkCaseChunkReassessmentByProduct batch identifies and reassesses all active product delivery cases for a given product, using chunker and stream jobs configurable by environment variables.

Explains how to identify and reassess all cases affected by system-wide data changes. It defines bulk reassessment requirements: complete consistency, efficiency, concurrency with online use, and business-specific control. It reviews reassessment behavior during a case’s lifetime, including when repeated reassessments do or do not change stored results. It compares top-down and bottom-up (Dependency Manager) approaches, alone or combined, and their trade-offs for missed/needless reassessments, ordering, extra processing, and handling changes made outside application APIs.

children.title: Writing your own Bulk Reassessment Batch Process
The CREOLEBulkCaseChunkReassessmentByProduct Batch Process
children.summary: Describes the built-in CREOLEBulkCaseChunkReassessmentByProduct batch process for bulk reassessment. It identifies and reassesses all active product delivery cases for a given product. The Chunker class takes a productID batch parameter and finds all cases for that product with status “Active”; the corresponding Stream class performs the reassessment. Also notes that batch behavior can be configured via environment variables. Useful if you need the standard reassessment mechanism rather than a custom bulk reassessment implementation.

children.title: Steps to Implement your own Bulk Reassessment Batch Process
children.summary: Explains how to implement a custom bulk reassessment batch process using Cúram’s chunked batch architecture. It requires a Chunker job to identify eligible cases (ensuring each case is selected once) and split them into chunks, and a Stream job to fully reassess each case in a chunk. The section also stresses defining shared data structures for metrics/reporting, such as cases processed and changed vs. unchanged determinations. It references related batch performance mechanisms and gives environment variables for the CREOLE Bulk Case Chunk Reassessment batch process.

Explains how to implement custom bulk reassessment using Cúram’s chunked batch architecture.
