If you need custom case selection or extra business processing at reassessment time, create a Chunker job to identify eligible cases exactly once and split them into chunks, and a Stream job to fully reassess each case. It also recommends tracking shared metrics like processed cases and changed vs. unchanged determinations, and warns that reassessment-time database writes can create precedent change items requiring Dependency Manager processing.

children.title: Bulk Reassessment for Multiple Simultaneous Changes
children.summary: Explains bulk reassessment when a single business change triggers multiple system-wide updates, such as CER rule set changes, splitting product lifetimes into new periods, capturing new evidence types mapped to new rule classes, and changing benefit rates. Notes additional batch-program concerns like metrics capture and error handling for chunked batch processing/skipping cases, but says this extra code is outside the guide’s scope. Includes a code example showing how to use DeterminationCalculator to reassess a case and check whether the decision changed.

children.title: Scheduling
children.summary: This section explains how to schedule bulk reassessment batch processing after system-wide data changes. It distinguishes regular scheduled execution from ad hoc execution triggered by publication events, recommends the Dependency Manager batch suite for pre-determined schedules, and warns against scheduling top-down algorithms this way because they reassess regardless of changes. It also covers chunked batch processing flexibility: scaling streamer instances, terminating/restarting streamers without losing uncompleted work, and pausing processing when machines are needed for other tasks.

Bulk Reassessment explains which system-wide changes require batch reassessment—product configuration, CER rule sets, rule object data configurations, and rate tables—and notes that changes are made in sandbox areas and take effect on publication or Apply Changes for rates. It outlines requirements for bulk processing: consistency, efficiency, concurrency, and business-specific control. It compares top-down vs Dependency Manager approaches, describes custom chunked batch implementation, and covers handling multiple simultaneous changes and scheduling options, including ad hoc vs scheduled execution and streamer scaling/pausing.

children.title: Diagnostics
children.summary: Explains Dependency Manager diagnostics for troubleshooting. It can log extra trace data to the DependencyTrace table and/or server log, enabled via curam.dependency.monitor.database and curam.dependency.monitor.log. Describes what each trace field means, the trace types (for example REASSESS, CADETER_SU/IN/UN, PCSBAT_SIZ/ERR, BATCH_FAIL, PCS_ADD), and notes that REASSESS and BATCH_FAIL always write to the table. Also shows how logged messages are formatted and gives sample log entries.

Covers reassessment when circumstances change: which case data changes trigger case-level reassessment, how the Dependency Manager finds affected dependents, and that reassessment may run in workflow or deferred processing. Explains Reassessment Aggregation, which serializes reassessment per case using ReassessmentQueue and ReassessmentQueueControl, is enabled by default, can be disabled with curam.case.reassessment.aggregation=NO, and falls back to Deferred To Batch on error. Also describes bulk reassessment for system-wide changes, scheduling/streamer options, and DependencyTrace diagnostics/logging.

children.title: 1.11 Incremental Design and Evolution
Introduction
children.summary: Introduces incremental design and evolution for Cúram Express Rules products. Explains that products can start from default rule sets, empty rule sets, or cloned rule sets from purchased solutions, then evolve as legislation and policy change. Highlights that CER rules are built from rule sets, rule classes, and rule attributes, and that rule sets are independent delivery units but may still have technical dependencies through contained artifacts. Useful for understanding initialization and cloning before opening deeper implementation guidance.

children.title: Starting with Rule Sets Included with the Application
How Rule Sets Inter-relate
CER Rules Artifacts - Technical Dependencies
children.summary: Explains technical dependencies among CER rules artifacts. CER rules are organized into rule sets, which contain rule classes, which contain rule attributes. Rule sets are the independently delivered unit, but dependencies can still exist through contained rule classes and attributes, both within a rule set and across rule sets. The section is relevant for understanding CER structure and the implications for cloning or customizing rule sets when starting from existing solutions.

children.title: CER Rules Artifacts - Logical Categorizations
children.summary: This section explains CER dependency types and logical categories. It distinguishes build-time dependencies, based on defined relationships, from runtime dependencies, which are free-form and need careful examination. It covers rule class single inheritance and rule attribute dependencies, including types, derived values, references, readall/create expressions, and lists across rule sets. It also introduces logical categorizations of rule classes as Data vs Derived Data/Business Logic, and as Infrastructure vs Application when provided out of the box.

Explains how CER rule sets interrelate for cloning/customization. CER rules are organized as rule sets containing rule classes and rule attributes; rule sets are delivered independently, but dependencies can still arise through contained artifacts within or across rule sets. Covers dependency types: build-time versus runtime, single inheritance between rule classes, and rule attribute dependencies via types, derived values, references, readall/create expressions, and lists. Also introduces logical categories of rule classes as Data vs Derived Data/Business Logic, and Infrastructure vs Application.

children.title: Cloning CER Rule Sets
children.summary: Explains how to clone application-included CER rule sets for customization, since in-place modification is not supported. It outlines identifying entry-point rule sets, tracing application rule-set dependencies forward and backward, then cloning all required application rule sets while excluding infrastructure rule classes. It also gives file-level steps for copying XML into the custom component and updating CREOLERuleSet.dmx and AppResource.dmx, plus notes on adding propagator configuration files in custom directories.

Introduces how to start from application-included CER rule sets and customize them safely by cloning, since in-place modification is not supported. Explains CER rule-set dependencies: build-time vs runtime, rule class single inheritance, and rule attribute dependencies via types, references, readall/create expressions, and lists. Distinguishes Data vs Derived Data/Business Logic rule classes, and Infrastructure vs Application rule classes. Gives the cloning process: identify entry points, trace application dependencies forward and backward, clone needed application rule sets, and update XML and .dmx files in the custom component.

children.title: Incremental Design
Choose Default Configuration Options for Your Product
children.summary: This section explains how to choose initial default configuration options for a CER-based product during incremental design. It recommends using shortcuts to get a product “up and running” before full implementation, while tracking technical debt and replacing shortcuts later. It notes that the key decision factors feature is deprecated. The main default options covered are enabling open-ended cases and setting the summarizer strategy to “Total weekly monetary entitlement.”

children.title: Implement a Single Product Period First
children.summary: Start with a single, open-ended product period even if you ultimately need multiple periods; add later periods only after the first is working, cloning its rule sets as needed. For the initial implementation, focus only on eligibility and entitlement rules, using simple placeholder logic if necessary, and avoid decision factors/details at first. In parallel, create reusable “data” rule classes for custom entities and evidence types, ideally in a separate rule set.

children.title: Focus on Eligibility/Entitlement Rules
children.summary: This section advises starting product implementation with a single, open-ended product period, then splitting into multiple periods later if needed. It says the core priority is eligibility and entitlement rules, not key decision factors or decision details. For an initial version, the rules may be hard-coded to fixed eligibility and entitlement outcomes until real logic is built. It also recommends working on single objectives first and, in parallel, creating reusable data rule classes for custom entities and evidence types in a separate rule set.

children.title: Spin-off a Task to Write Rule Classes for Custom Entities and/or Evidence Types
children.summary: This section advises parallelizing implementation: while one developer builds initial eligibility/entitlement rules, another can create “data” rule classes for custom entities and custom evidence types needed for calculations. It recommends keeping these reusable data rule classes in a separate rule set from eligibility/entitlement rule classes.

children.title: Top-down Implementations
children.summary: Explains a top-down approach for implementing eligibility/entitlement rules: start with hard-coded “always eligible” logic, then replace it with more detailed rule attributes, which may themselves begin as hard-coded before being implemented. Gives a Lone Parent benefit example decomposed into child age range, residency, lone parent, and means test checks. Also stresses earlier guidance on data rule classes: use correct data types, avoid calculated attributes except description, add navigation attributes later, and use rule object propagators when ready.

children.title: Bottom-up Implementations
children.summary: This section recommends “bottom-up” rule implementation: build and test calculator rule classes for complex propagated calculations before they are wired into eligibility/entitlement rules, then integrate and re-test later. It also suggests initially hard-coding rate data for faster development, with later refactoring to Cúram rate tables and propagator configuration; if shipped this way, future rate changes require rule-set changes and re-testing. Finally, it advises separating rule classes into distinct rule sets for data, eligibility/entitlement, decision details, and common calculators, with key decision factors noted as deprecated.

children.title: Hard-code Rates at First
children.summary: If rate data is not yet available as rate tables, you can hard-code rates directly in eligibility/entitlement rules and refactor later to Cúram rate tables, rate expressions, and propagator configuration. This is useful for initial implementation, but if hard-coded rates go live, future rate changes require rule set changes and re-testing. The section also advises keeping data rule classes, eligibility/entitlement rule classes, deprecated key decision factor classes, decision details classes, and common calculator classes in separate rule sets to improve reuse and isolate changes.

children.title: Keep an Eye on Rule Class Dependencies
children.summary: Advises separating rule classes into distinct rule sets to reduce re-testing and improve reuse. Specifically recommends separate sets for data rule classes, eligibility/entitlement rule classes, deprecated key decision factor rule classes, decision details rule classes, and common calculator rule classes. This helps isolate display-only or non-core changes from core eligibility logic. The section follows guidance on building bottom-up calculator rules and hard-coding rates initially, with a warning that hard-coded live rates require rule-set changes for future updates.

children.title: (deprecated) Try Key Decision Factors before Decision Details
children.summary: This section advises implementing key decision factors before decision details because key factors use a fixed data structure and built-in Engine screens, while decision details require free-form design and custom dynamic UIM screens. It suggests key decision factors as a simpler first step to show results are correct for the right reasons. It also recommends reusing the Basic Eligibility/Entitlement Decision Details display category first, including how to extend the basic case rule class and clone the related UIM/property files, before creating custom decision detail categories.

children.title: Re-use the Basic Decision Details before Writing Your Own
children.summary: Use the built-in Basic Eligibility/entitlement Decision Details before building custom decision-detail display categories. This helps you learn how decision details work and gives testers more explanation early, though the basic display can be replaced later. To reuse it, make your case rule class extend AbstractBasicProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractBasicCase, implement abstractCase to instantiate the underlying eligibility/entitlement rule class with productDeliveryCase, and clone the CREOLEDisplayRules_basicCaseDisplay and CREOLEDisplayRules_basicCaseDisplay_objectiveTagSubscreen UIM/properties files into your custom component.

children.title: Start Slowly with Decision Details
children.summary: Advises starting decision-detail implementation with simple dynamic UIM screens, avoiding subscreens, comparison data, and conditional display until rules reliably feed screens. If data is missing, use temporary hard-coded rule attributes, then replace them later. Also notes ongoing best practices: understand timelines, comment code as you go, test incrementally, use Cúram Solutions examples, refactor with unit-test support, and monitor logs for non-fatal warnings and errors.

children.title: Throughout Your Product's Development
children.summary: Guidance for developing with CER throughout the product lifecycle: understand timelines before building rule sets; comment rule classes and attributes as you implement; test incrementally with unit tests and then integration tests; study Cúram Solutions rule sets for patterns; refactor freely as better structures emerge; and monitor application logs for non-fatal warnings or errors because the engine often continues despite configuration issues.

Guidance for incrementally designing CER-based products: start with shortcuts to get a product working early, but track technical debt. Use default setup options like open-ended cases, “Total weekly monetary entitlement,” compare all user-facing data, and no reassessment of closed cases. Begin with one open-ended product period, hard-code simple eligibility/entitlement results if needed, and defer key decision factors and decision details. Build reusable data rule classes in separate rule sets, consider top-down or bottom-up implementation, hard-code rates only temporarily, separate rule sets by function, and test/refactor as you go.

children.title: Handling Legislation Change
Branching Logic in Your CER Rule Sets
children.summary: This section compares two ways to handle legislation changes in CER rule sets: add branching logic with the legislationChange expression, or split a product into multiple product periods with separate rule sets. It explains when each approach is appropriate, including that many changed attributes may favor product periods. It also notes that either approach requires updating and possibly cloning tests, and that publishing rule set changes can trigger bulk reassessment of existing cases.

children.title: Multiple Product Periods for Your Product
children.summary: This section explains the “multiple product periods” approach for handling legislation changes in CER. It describes carving a product’s lifetime into distinct periods, each with its own eligibility/entitlement rule sets. It contrasts this with branching logic in rules and notes that multiple product periods are useful when many rule attributes would otherwise need branching. It also covers cloning existing rule sets, limiting cloning to affected rules, renaming/refactoring as needed, and cloning/updating tests to verify changes.

children.title: Choosing the Right Approach
children.summary: Compares when to use branching logic versus multiple product periods for legislation changes. Key factors include testing effort, refactoring, maintenance, complexity, stability of old determinations, display categories, and whether all cases change on the same date. Branching logic suits small, localized changes; multiple product periods suit wider changes and guarantee older periods remain unaffected. The section also notes performance is usually similar, discusses registration-date and transitional-period edge cases, and warns that the key decision factors feature is deprecated.

Explains how CER handles legislation changes when rules must shift from old to new law. Two supported approaches are covered: add branching logic in rule attributes using the legislationChange expression, or split the product into multiple product periods with cloned rule sets. It outlines cloning and testing impacts, bulk reassessment on publish, and a comparison of tradeoffs: branching suits small localized changes; multiple product periods suit wider changes and better isolate old determinations. Also discusses display categories, registration-date/transitional edge cases, and that performance is usually similar.

children.title: Changing Product Configuration Settings
Decision Summary Display Strategy
children.summary: This section explains the product-level “decision summary display strategy” setting and its impact. It states that the strategy controls how each coverage period is summarized for case workers, and that changing it affects display of all determinations for the product, including historical and future ones. The section is part of a broader discussion of configurable product settings, contrasting this display strategy with other settings that only affect new future cases or determinations.

children.title: Determination Comparison Strategy
children.summary: Explains that product-level configuration settings can change over time and affect behavior differently for future and historical data. For determination comparison strategy, the product setting controls how different a new assessment must be to be stored, and changes apply only to new determinations created after the change; existing stored determinations are unaffected. The section contrasts this with decision summary display and open-ended case settings, noting which changes impact historical records versus only future cases.

children.title: Allow Open-Ended Cases
children.summary: Explains the product-level “allow open-ended cases” setting, which controls whether the Engine can create a new case without an expected or actual end date. Changing this setting only affects new cases created in the future; existing stored cases are unchanged. A product may later disallow open-ended cases while still containing older open-ended cases created when the setting was enabled.

children.title: Reassessment Strategy
children.summary: Defines how the Engine decides which cases can be reassessed. Two options are supported: reassess all cases, or do not reassess closed cases (default for new products). The choice affects dependency-record creation and performance, especially with many cases. Changing the setting triggers the ApplyProductReassessmentStrategy batch job to update existing cases; published product changes warn that the batch job must run before the new strategy applies. Also notes compliance guidance and that the eligibility/entitlement public API is customer-facing and stable under impact standards.

This section lists product-level settings that can be changed after cases already exist: decision summary display strategy, determination comparison strategy, allow open-ended cases, and reassessment strategy. It explains that some changes affect all determinations, including historical ones, while others apply only to future determinations or cases. Decision summary display changes the presentation of coverage periods; comparison strategy changes when determinations are stored; open-ended cases controls creation of cases without end dates.

Guidance for evolving CER-based products over time. Covers starting with default, empty, or cloned rule sets; how rule sets, rule classes, and rule attributes depend on each other; and how to clone application rule sets safely because in-place modification is unsupported. Describes incremental implementation shortcuts, default product settings, and using a single open-ended product period first. Also explains handling legislation change via branching logic or multiple product periods, plus which product configuration settings can be changed later and their effects on existing vs future cases.

children.title: 1.12 Compliancy for eligibility and entitlement
The Public API
Identifying the Public API
children.summary: This section defines how to identify the Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure public API: the JavaDoc for restricted code packages is the sole source of truth. It lists multiple internal `curam.core.facade.infrastructure.*` and `curam.core.sl.infrastructure.*` packages that customers must not extend, implement, or add custom classes to unless JavaDoc explicitly allows it. It also notes two exception code tables, `CaseAssessmentDetReason` and `CaseSnapshotDetReason`, where customers may add custom codes.

Defines the Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure public API: customer application code may invoke it, and it is protected from breaking changes without customer-impact procedures. This section is mainly a compliance/compatibility note signaling which APIs are safe to use and stable over time.

children.title: Code Package Restrictions
children.summary: This section defines code package restrictions for Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure. It lists internal restricted Java packages whose public APIs are identified only by JavaDoc; customers must not implement, subclass, or add custom classes/interfaces there unless explicitly allowed. It also highlights two customizable code tables—CaseAssessmentDetReason and CaseSnapshotDetReason—where customers may add custom codes using the recommended approach.

children.title: Code Table Restrictions
CaseAssessmentDetReason
children.summary: Describes the CaseAssessmentDetReason code table in the assessment.codetable package, which records why an assessment occurred (for example, Case Activation or Change of Evidence). Unlike other code tables in the package, customers may add custom codes using the recommended approach. This section also sits within broader guidance on restricted internal packages and public API identification for Eligibility and Entitlement infrastructure.

children.title: CaseSnapshotDetReason
children.summary: Defines the CaseSnapshotDetReason code table in `curam.core.sl.infrastructure.assessment.codetable`, used to record why a case snapshot was taken (for example, case approval or rejection). Unlike most code tables in the package, customers may add custom codes using the recommended approach. The section also notes that public API identification depends on JavaDoc for restricted packages.

children.title: Restricted Code Table Packages
children.summary: This section lists restricted code table packages and code tables reserved for the Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure. It states that customers must not modify these tables or create custom versions/additional codes. It also introduces database restrictions for infrastructure tables, emphasizing they are internal and accessible only through the public API. Finally, it describes the RuleObjectPropagatorControl table as a single-row internal control table used to manage initial CREOLE rule object propagation, which must not be read or written by other components.

children.title: Restricted Code Tables
children.summary: Lists restricted Cúram code tables and database restrictions for Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure. It names restricted code table packages and specific `curam.codetable` entries that customers must not modify or extend, except `CaseAssessmentDetReason` and `CaseSnapshotDetReason`. It also explains that internal database tables are accessible only via public API, and highlights `RuleObjectPropagatorControl` as a single-row internal control table used to ensure initial rule-object propagation runs once during Guice initialization.

Defines the restricted Eligibility and Entitlement code-table rules. It states that JavaDoc is the sole source for identifying the public API in restricted code packages, and customers must not add custom classes or implementations there. For code tables, most `curam.codetable` entries are fixed, but `CaseAssessmentDetReason` and `CaseSnapshotDetReason` in `curam.core.sl.infrastructure.assessment.codetable` are customisable via the recommended approach. It also points to internal database restrictions, including the single-row `RuleObjectPropagatorControl` table used during initial rule propagation.

children.title: Database Restrictions
RuleObjectPropagatorControl
children.summary: Describes the RuleObjectPropagatorControl database table, a single-row internal control table used to manage initial propagation of application data to CREOLE rule objects. The row records whether initial propagation has already run, ensuring exactly one application server instance performs it during Guice initialization. The table is part of the Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure and must not be read or written by other components.

children.title: Restricted Database Tables
children.summary: Lists the database tables reserved for the Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure and warns that customers must not modify the DMX file populating RuleObjectPropagatorControl or create other DMX files targeting it. The section is mainly a reference list of restricted tables such as BreakdownInfo, CaseDecision, CREOLEProduct*, Reassessment*, and payment correction/over-underpayment tables. Useful for identifying protected schema objects that should not be altered directly.

Explains database restrictions for the Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure. It states that these tables are internal and generally accessible only through the public API. The section details RuleObjectPropagatorControl, a single-row control table used during initial CREOLE rule object propagation, whose row is set once during Guice initialization and must not be read or written by other components. It also lists restricted database tables such as BreakdownInfo, CaseDecision, CREOLEProduct*, Reassessment*, and payment correction/over-underpayment tables, and warns against altering the related DMX file.

children.title: CER Rule Sets Included with the Application
children.summary: The section says CER rule sets delivered with the application must not be modified in place. If you need to base custom logic on them, you must clone the rule sets, including dependent and precedent sets, into your custom component. It also notes that certain database tables may be initially populated via DMX files, but most cannot.

Defines compliance rules for the Eligibility and Entitlement Infrastructure. It states the public API is safe to use and protected from breaking changes, and that JavaDoc is the only source for identifying public API in restricted packages. It lists restricted code packages and code tables, allowing custom codes only for CaseAssessmentDetReason and CaseSnapshotDetReason. It also covers internal database restrictions, including the single-row RuleObjectPropagatorControl table and other reserved tables, and warns that bundled CER rule sets must not be modified in place.

children.title: 1.13 The Eligibility and Entitlement Engine API and Customizability
Eligibility and Entitlement Engine API
children.summary: Defines the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine API as the public entry point for eligibility and entitlement determination. The API consists of AssessmentEngine, AssessmentEngineImpl, AssessmentEngineEntity, and AssessmentEngineEntityImpl in the assessment.impl package. It explicitly forbids providing custom implementations of the Java interfaces or subclassing the implementation classes. This section is useful if you need the supported integration points or customization limits for the engine.

children.title: Customizability
Eligibility and Entitlement Engine events
children.summary: This section lists Eligibility and Entitlement Engine customization points. It describes available business events, including AssessmentEngineEvent and ProductDeliveryLifeCycleEvents, which cover lifecycle stages such as approval, activation, closure, reactivation, and rejection, and notes that customers can bind custom implementations via Guice. It also lists hook classes, including AssessmentEngineHooks and ReassessEligiblityHook, for supplying custom input during eligibility assessment. Useful if you need event-driven extensions or hook-based customization in the engine.

children.title: Eligibility and Entitlement Engine Hooks
children.summary: This section explains customization points in the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine. It covers business events that let customers add logic at key stages, including `AssessmentEngineEvent` and `ProductDeliveryLifeCycleEvents`, and notes that `ProductDeliveryLifeCycleEvents` can be supplied via Guice bindings. It also describes hook classes for custom input and assessment behavior, especially `AssessmentEngineHooks` and `ReassessEligiblityHook`. Useful for finding extension points, event-based integration, and Javadoc references for engine customization.

children.title: Product Delivery Effective Date Hook
children.summary: Explains how to override the default product delivery effective date set during approval by implementing `curam.core.hook.impl.ProductDeliveryApprovalStrategyHook`. The default uses the approval date plus frequency pattern to determine the effective date, which then serves as the financial anchor date for payment calculations. Includes a sample custom hook class with `setEffectiveDate(caseID, approvalDate)` and notes that the implementation must be bound in a Guice module to replace the default behavior.

This section outlines customizability points in the Eligibility and Entitlement Engine. It covers business events such as `AssessmentEngineEvent` and `ProductDeliveryLifeCycleEvents`, which can be extended via Guice bindings, and hook classes including `AssessmentEngineHooks` and `ReassessEligiblityHook` for custom assessment input and behavior. It also explains how to override the default product delivery effective date during approval using `ProductDeliveryApprovalStrategyHook`, including its role as the financial anchor date and the need to bind a custom implementation in Guice.

Defines the public Eligibility and Entitlement Engine API entry points (AssessmentEngine, AssessmentEngineImpl, AssessmentEngineEntity, AssessmentEngineEntityImpl) and states they must not be replaced or subclassed. Describes customization via business events and hooks: AssessmentEngineEvent, ProductDeliveryLifeCycleEvents, AssessmentEngineHooks, and ReassessEligiblityHook, all extendable through Guice bindings. Also explains ProductDeliveryApprovalStrategyHook for overriding the default product delivery effective date, which acts as the financial anchor date for payments.

children.title: 1.14 Extensions to Cúram Express Rules
Introduction
children.summary: Introduction to Cúram Express Rules extensions for eligibility/entitlement processing. Notes that the Engine adds expressions and annotations usable only for case eligibility and entitlement, not other purposes. This section starts the expression list: combineSuccessionSets, legislationChange, and rate. It then introduces combineSuccessionSets, which merges non-overlapping succession sets into one history with default values for gaps, useful for tracking changing periods such as a person’s absence from a household.

children.title: Expressions
combineSuccessionSets
Overview
children.summary: Introduces Cúram Express Rules extensions contributed by the Engine for eligibility/entitlement processing only, including combineSuccessionSets, legislationChange, and rate. The section then begins the combineSuccessionSets overview: it is used with Active Succession Set Rule Object Converter to splice together non-overlapping succession sets into one history, filling gaps with default values. Example context is a person’s absence from household, where each absence period has start/end dates and a reason that may change during the absence.

children.title: Example
children.summary: Introduces the CER extension expression `combineSuccessionSets`, used only for eligibility/entitlement processing. It “splices together” multiple non-overlapping succession sets into one history with default values filling gaps. The example uses a person’s absence evidence: separate absence periods can have changing reasons over time, and the combined result tracks a single absence history. A detailed John Smith scenario shows three absence periods and how the reason attribute changes across dates such as Education, Medical Treatment, Incarceration, and Military.

children.title: Detailed Behavior
children.summary: Describes `combineSuccessionSets` behavior: it takes one list of `ActiveSuccessionSet` rule objects and a nominated return rule class, then creates a return rule object whose attributes are matched by name from the inputs. Gaps in timelines use defaults (`0`, `false`, null code-table entry, or `null`), and if the input list is empty those defaults apply across all timelines. Also lists validation rules: input list must be annotated with `SuccessionSetPopulation`, and the return class must be non-abstract, uninitialized, and have matching derived attributes.

children.title: CER Editor reference
children.summary: Describes runtime validation for combineSuccessionSets: each contributing succession set must have a non-blank start date and must not overlap with others, with at most one open-ended set, or an exception is thrown. Also gives CER Editor implementation steps for a CombineSuccessionSet example: create an Absence succession set rule class (start/end dates, reason), define a CombinedAbsence return class, and create a Person rule class to hold the absence list.

children.title: XML Reference
children.summary: Provides an XML reference for the `combineSuccessionSets` expression in Cúram Express Rules. It explains required XML structure: a `ruleclass` attribute, optional `ruleset`, and one child expression returning a list of succession set rule objects. The sample XML defines `Person`, `CombinedAbsence`, and `Absence` rule classes, showing how to read absence evidence, combine absence succession sets, and derive a Boolean eligibility timeline based on absence reason.

Introduces the CER-only `combineSuccessionSets` expression, which merges multiple non-overlapping succession sets into one history and fills gaps with defaults (`0`, `false`, null code-table entry, or `null`). Uses a person’s absence history as the main example, including changing absence reasons over time. Explains validation and runtime rules: input must be a `SuccessionSetPopulation`, sets cannot overlap, and the return class must match derived attributes. Also shows CER Editor setup and XML structure for `Person`, `Absence`, and `CombinedAbsence`.

children.title: legislationChange
Overview
children.summary: Explains the `legislationChange` expression in Cúram Express Rules for handling legislative changes by branching rule logic across “eras.” Uses an eligibility example for “low-waged” status that changes over time from pre-tax to post-tax income thresholds. Defines required behavior: specify the timeline data type, include an initial era plus later eras, and ensure all eras return the same value type. When evaluated, era timelines are spliced by era dates and contiguous identical values are merged.

children.title: Example
children.summary: Introduces the Cúram Express Rules legislationChange expression for handling legislative branching over time. It explains using multiple legislation eras, including an initial era from the start of time, to combine different rule implementations into one timeline. An example shows eligibility for “low-waged” changing from pre-tax income under $20,000, to post-tax under $15,000 in 2001, then to broader pre/post-tax thresholds in 2002. Notes that era timelines are spliced by effective dates and contiguous identical values are merged.

children.title: Detailed Behavior
children.summary: Explains the detailed behavior of the `legislationChange` expression in Cúram Express Rules. It supports branching rule logic across legislation eras to model changes over time. Each instance must declare the timeline interval data type and include an initial era plus one or more subsequent eras, all returning the same value type. When evaluated, era timelines are spliced by era dates into one overall timeline, with identical contiguous values merged. If a legislation change doesn’t affect the result, the output timeline remains unchanged.

children.title: CER Editor reference
children.summary: Explains the CER Editor’s Legislation Change element and its XML reference. It shows how to represent legislation-driven logic as a timeline, with an intervaltype and one or more era elements, each containing a from date expression and a value timeline expression. The section also outlines an example implementation: creating a Person rule class with caseParticipantRoleID, preTaxIncomeTimeline, postTaxIncomeTimeline, and isEligibleTimeline, then deriving eligibility across three legislation eras. Notes that hard-coded cut-off rates are only for illustration; production rules should use rate tables.

children.title: XML Reference
children.summary: This section explains the XML representation of the legislationChange rule element in Cúram Express Rules. It defines the required structure: an intervaltype and one or more era elements, each with a from date expression and a value timeline expression. It then shows a full XML example for a Person rule class with preTaxIncomeTimeline, postTaxIncomeTimeline, and isEligibleTimeline, illustrating how eligibility logic changes across legislation eras starting from null, 2001-01-01, and 2002-01-01.

Explains the `legislationChange` expression in Cúram Express Rules for branching rule logic across legislation eras over time. It requires a timeline interval type, an initial era from the start of time, and one or more later eras returning the same value type. Evaluated era timelines are spliced by effective dates, with contiguous identical values merged. Includes a worked eligibility example (“low-waged”) and XML/CER Editor implementation showing era-based Boolean eligibility derived from pre-tax and post-tax income timelines.

children.title: rate
Overview
children.summary: Introduces the rate expression in Cúram Express Rules. It works with the Rate Rule Object Propagator to create rule objects from rate table cells and lets rules look up the correct rate for a given table cell. The section explains why rate tables are used instead of hard-coding values: changing an eligibility limit, such as an income threshold, can be done by updating the rate table without changing or retesting the rule set. Includes an example of time-varying income and income limits.

children.title: Example
children.summary: Introduces the rate expression in Cúram Express Rules, which lets a rules designer look up the rule object for a specific cell in a rate table. Explains why rate tables are used for values like income limits that change over time without changing the rule set, avoiding rule retesting. Uses an eligibility example where a person’s total income is compared against a periodically revised income limit, with income and limit values changing over several dates.

children.title: Detailed Behavior
children.summary: This section explains how a rate expression resolves its value from a rate table cell using three coordinates: rate table code, row code, and column code. It describes versioned rate tables, where the returned rate is a timeline of the cell’s values across table versions, with unchanged adjacent values merged. It also covers fallback behavior when propagation has not run: the rate creates an internal rule object, enabling unit tests, and later recalculates to use the external propagated rule when available.

children.title: CER Editor reference
children.summary: This section explains CER rate expressions and the Rate Table element in the CER Editor. It notes that any evaluation of rate expressions requires an active database transaction, and that unit tests using them must either run in a transaction (for example via CuramServerTest) or avoid evaluation by overriding values with CER “specify.” It also outlines a sample implementation using a Person rule class with totalIncomeTimeline and isEligibleTimeline, then describes the XML reference for rate instances (table, row, and column code table attributes).

children.title: XML Reference
children.summary: This section documents the XML representation of CER rate expressions. It explains that each rate instance includes table, row, and column attributes, which reference codes from the RateTableType, RateRowType, and RateColumnType code tables. It also notes that rate expressions require an active database transaction when evaluated, especially in unit tests, and that tests can avoid evaluation by using CER “specify” overrides.

Explains the CER `rate` expression for looking up values in rate tables via table, row, and column codes. It describes how the Rate Rule Object Propagator creates matching rule objects, how rate timelines reflect versioned table values, and how unchanged adjacent versions are merged. It also covers fallback behavior when propagation has not run, allowing internal rule objects for tests. The section notes that evaluating rates requires an active database transaction, with unit-test workarounds using `CuramServerTest` or CER `specify`.

Section covers CER expressions added by the Engine: combineSuccessionSets, legislationChange, and rate. combineSuccessionSets merges non-overlapping succession sets into one history with default gap values, strict validation, and a Person/Absence/CombinedAbsence example. legislationChange branches timeline logic by effective legislation eras and merges contiguous identical values. rate looks up versioned rate-table cells by table/row/column codes, supports fallback internal objects if propagation is absent, and requires a database transaction for evaluation.

children.title: Annotations
Display
XML Reference
children.summary: Shows the XML reference for the `Display` annotation in Cúram Express Rules. It explains that `Display` marks a rule attribute for inclusion in decision details when the engine gathers decision data, and can only be applied to rule attributes. The section includes an example `RuleSet` XML snippet with `Display` on `dateOfBirth` and `fullName` attributes, illustrating how displayable fields are defined and derived in XML.

Defines the Cúram Express Rules `Display` annotation. It marks a rule attribute for inclusion in decision details when the engine gathers data for a determination, and it can only be applied to rule attributes. The XML example shows a `RuleSet` with `Display` applied to `dateOfBirth` and `fullName`, illustrating how displayable fields are declared and derived in XML.

children.title: DisplaySubscreen
XML Reference
children.summary: Explains the XML Reference for the DisplaySubscreen annotation in Cúram Express Rules. It shows how to mark rule attributes for decision-details subscreens, usable only on rule attributes or classes extending ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCase. Includes a full example with a main case class listing household members, a dynamiclist-derived subscreen wrapper class, and display attributes such as totalIncome. Useful for understanding how expandable decision details are structured in XML.

Defines the DisplaySubscreen annotation for Cúram Express Rules. It marks rule attributes that return data for decision-details subscreens shown when a user expands a row in the Engine UI. It can be used only on rule attributes or on classes extending ProductDecisionDetailsRuleSet.AbstractCase. The section includes XML examples: a main case with a displayed householdMembers list, a dynamiclist-derived householdMemberSubscreens wrapper, and a subscreen class exposing display data like totalIncome.

children.title: Legislation
XML Reference
children.summary: This section introduces the XML reference for the CER “Legislation” annotation. It explains that legislation links can be attached to a rule set, rule class, rule attribute, or expression, and that links may be absolute (http/https) or relative to the application. It includes an example XML RuleSet showing these cases. Useful if you need the exact XML structure and linking rules for adding legislation annotations.

Defines the CER “Legislation” annotation, which links a rule element to an arbitrary HTML document describing the legislation behind it. It can be attached to a rule set, rule class, rule attribute, or expression. The section also explains link behavior in the CER Editor: http/https values open as absolute URLs, while all other values are treated as paths relative to the application. Includes XML examples showing absolute and relative legislation links.

children.title: SuccessionSetPopulation
XML Reference
children.summary: Provides XML for the SuccessionSetPopulation annotation in a rule set. It shows an example rule class extending PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveSuccessionSet, with startDateAttribute and endDateAttribute set to Date-valued attributes. The section explains that the annotation applies only to rule classes, the named attributes must exist and return Date values, and null or omitted dates imply “beginning of time” or “until the beginning of time” behavior for the succession set.

Defines the SuccessionSetPopulation annotation for Active Succession Set Rule Object Converter. It applies only to rule classes that ultimately extend PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveSuccessionSet, and lets you name optional start and end date attributes on the class. Those attributes must exist and return Date values. Missing or null start dates mean “from the beginning of time”; missing or null end dates mean “until the beginning of time.” Includes XML example showing startDate and terminationDate on an Employment rule class.

children.title: relatedEvidence
XML Reference
children.summary: This section documents the XML `relatedEvidence` annotation for Cúram Express Rules. It explains that, when Active Evidence Row Rule Objects are populated, this annotation automatically populates a rule attribute with related evidence rule objects. It applies only to attributes on rule classes ultimately extending `PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveEvidenceRow`, and the attribute type must be either that rule class or a `List` of it. An XML example shows parent/child evidence rows being populated by the Active Evidence Row Rule Object Propagator.

Defines the XML `relatedEvidence` annotation for Cúram Express Rules. It is used with Active Evidence Row Rule Objects to automatically populate a rule attribute with related evidence rule objects during conversion processing. The annotation applies only to attributes on rule classes that ultimately extend `PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveEvidenceRow`, and the attribute type must be either that rule class or a `List` of it. Includes an XML example showing parent/child evidence row propagation.

children.title: relatedSuccessionSet
XML Reference
children.summary: Defines the XML reference for the `relatedSuccessionSet` annotation. It explains that, when Active Succession Set rule objects are populated, the annotated attribute is automatically filled with related evidence rule objects. The section lists constraints: the annotation applies only to rule attributes, the attribute’s class must ultimately extend `PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveSuccessionSet`, and its return type must be either such a rule class or a `List` of one. Includes an XML example using `HouseholdMember` and the Active Succession Set Rule Object Propagator.

Defines the `relatedSuccessionSet` XML annotation for rule attributes that should be auto-populated with related evidence rule objects when Active Succession Set rule objects are converted. It applies only to rule attributes, and the annotated class must ultimately extend `PropagatorRuleSet.ActiveSuccessionSet`. The attribute type must be either a matching Active Succession Set rule class or a `List` of one. Includes XML example usage with `HouseholdMember` and the Active Succession Set Rule Object Propagator.

Section covering Cúram Express Rules annotations introduced by the Engine: Display marks rule attributes for decision-details inclusion; DisplaySubscreen marks attributes or AbstractCase-based classes that supply expandable subscreen details; Legislation links rule sets/classes/attributes/expressions to HTML legislation pages with absolute or relative URL behavior; SuccessionSetPopulation names start/end date attributes for ActiveSuccessionSet conversion; relatedEvidence and relatedSuccessionSet auto-populate linked evidence/succession-set objects on matching rule attributes with type/extends restrictions. Includes XML examples for each.

Section introduces Engine extensions to Cúram Express Rules for eligibility/entitlement only. It covers combineSuccessionSets, which merges non-overlapping succession sets into one timeline with default gap values and strict validation; legislationChange, which branches by legislation eras; and rate, which reads versioned rate-table cells and requires a database transaction. It also documents annotations: Display, DisplaySubscreen, Legislation, SuccessionSetPopulation, relatedEvidence, and relatedSuccessionSet, including their placement rules, type restrictions, and XML examples.

children.title: 1.15 Caching
children.summary: Explains EvidenceDescriptor caching during reassessment transactions: when a case ID can be inferred from a search, all EvidenceDescriptor records for that case are loaded into the transaction cache, and later case-related searches are served from cache instead of the database. The cache applies only to reassessment transactions, not online evidence edits/modifications. It is enabled by default and can be disabled with the application property `curam.evidence.evdescripcache.disabled=TRUE`.

children.title: 1.16 Environment Variables
Environment Variables Governing Behavior of Engine
children.summary: Lists Engine environment variables that control tracing, rule object propagation, batch reassessment, workflow ticket generation, case assessment, evidence caching, and reassessment behavior. Includes flags and tunables for logging levels, error handling, chunk sizes and retry intervals, aggregation of reassessments, cache disabling, and suppression of specific evidence methods. Also notes default values for some settings and points to related documentation for logging, caching, batch processes, and reassessment.

Lists Engine environment variables for controlling tracing/logging, rule object propagation errors, case determination warnings, batch CREOLE bulk case chunk reassessment settings (chunk size, retry intervals, stream behavior, unprocessed chunks), workflow ticket generation on eligibility failure or successful reassessment, product publication and case assessment tracing, rule object snapshot storage, propagator loading suppression, reassessment aggregation and wait period, evidence cache disabling, and suppression overrides for specific evidence methods. Includes defaults for some flags and pointers to related logging, caching, batch, and reassessment docs.

Introduces the guide to developing with Cúram Express Rules for eligibility and entitlement. It explains the Engine’s purpose, audience, related documents, and a staged approach from basic results to detailed explanations. The section also previews the full chapter structure: end-to-end processing, viewing determinations, calculating/displaying results, converters and propagators, storage, financial scheduling, reassessment, design evolution, compliance, API/customization, CER extensions, caching, and environment variables. Deprecated key decision factors are noted.
